## Exp 224 — M4b clean re-run with the AFFECT-specific proposer: the autopilot now runs END-TO-END (clean rc=0 after fixing 4 instrument issues), and at N=2 finds NO improvement — but CRITIC-GATED: both proposer prior-changes were rejected by the AffectClaudeCritic BEFORE the FROZEN scorer ever measured them, so improvability is untested by the scorer (NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT; blind-verified)

- Plain: After fixing the Exp 223 problems (own affect mission, its own notes file, a longer 600s timeout)
  AND a second bug found mid-run (the affect notes folder got wiped every round by git branch-switching),
  the auto-improver finally completed a clean 2-round run. Result: it did NOT improve the agent — but not
  because the changes scored worse. Both of Claude's proposed model tweaks were REJECTED by the reviewer
  step before they were ever graded. So the bottleneck at this scale is the REVIEWER (critic), not the
  scorer: the metric was never measured on any proposed change. A leftover hygiene bug also means the notes
  file only kept the last round's entry, and the clone is gone so we can't read WHY the reviewer rejected.
  No improvement found; the autopilot machinery is proven to run end-to-end. Functional valence only; no
  sentience claim.
- Hypothesis (predeclared, with named falsifier): with the affect-specific proposer (own mission + isolated
  world_model_affect + 600s timeout) the M4b autopilot completes a clean bounded run AND can RAISE the genuine
  learns-to-positive metric above baseline (0.4225) by mutating build_direct_head_model's priors, without
  reward-hacking. FALSIFIER: if BOTH iterations COMPLETE and no proposal both improves the metric AND holds
  verdict (all discarded as no_improvement / guardrail_fail / critic_reject / tests_failed), NEGATIVE (no gain
  at N=2). A claude/scorer failure (timeout/crash) is STILL an INSTRUMENT failure.
- Setup: run_affect_loop.py --real --iterations 2 on an isolated clone (HEAD d01f96d). --real = AffectClaudeProposer
  (affect mission, reads world_model_affect/INDEX.md, 600s timeout) + AffectClaudeCritic (affect review prompt)
  + the FROZEN score_affect() (300t x 8 seeds, ~10 min/candidate). KEEP iff metric>best AND verdict. Two instrument
  bugs were fixed to reach a clean run: Exp 223's (timeout + lang context) and this run's mid-flight discovery —
  the untracked world_model_affect dir was WIPED each iteration (commit_all sweeps it onto the proposal branch,
  then discard->checkout(trunk) removes it since it is not tracked on trunk, unlike the lang world_model). Fixed
  (re-mkdir each iteration) + guarded (tests/test_affect_pr_loop.py::test_run_affect_pr_loop_completes_and_writes_isolated_journal).
- Result: VERDICT NEGATIVE (falsifier fires). returncode=0 — CLEAN completion ("Affect PR loop finished"); no
  crash/timeout (the 600s timeout + the dir fix held). Baseline real score reproduced = 0.4225. Both iterations
  (i=0, i=1) executed; the proposer produced valid affect_spec.py mutations ("affect proposal applied") that
  passed frozen-guard + the affect tests, but the AffectClaudeCritic REJECTED both -> critic_reject -> neither
  candidate was ever scored. 0 merges; best stayed at baseline 0.4225; no frozen path touched. (Residual: a
  journal data-loss bug persisted only the LAST iteration's row — world_model_affect is still wiped each round,
  so append starts a fresh file; my fix stopped the CRASH but not the data loss. The clone is deleted, so the
  critic_reason text is not recoverable.)
- Implication: a real but PARTIAL milestone — the M4b autopilot is now proven to run END-TO-END for a clean
  bounded real session (after fixing FOUR instrument issues from repurposing the lang autopilot: short timeout,
  lang mission context, shared world_model, untracked-dir git wipe). The first science datum is NEGATIVE, and
  its character is the NEW INSIGHT: the BINDING gate at this scale is the CRITIC, not the scorer — both
  proposer prior-tweaks were rejected before measurement, so the FROZEN scorer never tested whether they
  improve the metric. So "the baseline priors are improvable by the autopilot" is NOT answered: the loop failed
  UPSTREAM of scoring. Two readings, indistinguishable without the critic_reason: the critic correctly rejected
  unsound prior-hacks, OR the critic (an LLM soft gate on a tiny-diff prior change) is mis-calibrated / too
  strict. Tier: a process/instrument result + a gate-localization finding; no improvability verdict on the
  metric (the scorer never ran on a mutant).
- Honest caveat: NEGATIVE per the predeclared falsifier (both iterations complete, 0 improving merges), but it
  is CRITIC-GATED, not score-driven — NO mutant was ever scored, so this is NOT evidence the metric is
  unimprovable; it is evidence the critic blocked the proposer at N=2. Residual instrument issues remain: (a)
  the journal accumulation bug (only the last row persists); (b) the critic_reason is unrecorded (can't judge
  if the rejections were sound); (c) the AffectClaudeCritic + AffectClaudeProposer (real claude -p) are not
  unit-tested (like the lang ClaudeCli classes). N=2 is tiny; the lever is narrow (model priors only — the
  FROZEN scorer fixes optimism/lr/gamma-schedule). Functional valence only; no sentience claim.
- Verdict: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT. Self-grade: NEGATIVE — the predeclared falsifier fired (clean 2-iteration
  completion, 0 improving merges); the new insight is the gate localization (critic-gated, scorer never ran on
  a mutant) + the end-to-end-runs milestone, not an improvability claim.
- Verifier: agree — an independent blinded agent applied the predeclared rule: 0 merges with metric>0.4225;
  both iterations completed cleanly (rc=0, no crash/timeout); both proposals critic_reject (an explicitly named
  discard reason) -> falsifier fires -> NEGATIVE; and classified the failure mode as CRITIC-GATED (candidates
  rejected before scoring; the metric was never measured on any mutant), not score-driven.
- Next (CONSULT — a human word): (a) a CLEANER re-run — fix the journal-accumulation bug + capture the
  critic_reason + inspect/calibrate the AffectClaudeCritic (is it correctly rejecting unsound prior-hacks, or
  too strict?), optionally more iterations, to get an actual scorer-measured improvability datum; (b) ACCEPT
  the M4b finding as-is — the autopilot runs end-to-end but is critic-gated at this scale; the M4a deliverable
  (converse REPL + the FROZEN unfakeable scorer, Exp 222) stands as the milestone; (c) pivot to the short-session
  LEARNING lever (Exp 221). Guards: tests/test_affect_pr_loop.py; card: loop/directions/affective-dyad.md.
- trace: experiments/exp224_m4b_clean_run.py → experiments/outputs/exp224.txt

## Exp 225 — M4b critic diagnostic + scoring the critic-approved direction: the M4b critic is CORRECTLY CONSERVATIVE (rejects baking code→intent into A0 = gaming; approves a C1 NEU-aversion preference), resolving Exp 224's ambiguity — AND that approved, non-gaming change GENUINELY IMPROVES the metric (0.4225→0.5188, genuine 6/8→8/8) ⇒ the autopilot has a real improving move; Exp 224's NEGATIVE was N=2 sampling, not a ceiling (POSITIVE / NEW INSIGHT; blind-verified)

- Plain: Exp 224 left a question: when the auto-improver found NO improvement, was that because the
  reviewer (critic) correctly blocked bad changes, or because the reviewer is too strict? This answers it
  cheaply. First we watched the reviewer judge 3 of Claude's proposed model changes: it REJECTED two that
  tried to secretly hand the agent the answer (hard-coding which signal means which intent — cheating the
  "learn it yourself" rule) and APPROVED one honest one (a mild preference tweak so the agent commits to a
  reply that earns approval instead of dithering on a neutral "ask"). So the reviewer is doing its job, not
  being too strict. Then we SCORED that approved tweak: it genuinely improves the agent — approval rate
  0.42→0.52 AND the can't-be-faked discrimination 6/8→8/8 (better than the milestone). So the auto-improver
  DOES have a real, honest winning move; Exp 224's "no improvement" was just bad luck in 2 tries (both were
  the cheating kind, correctly rejected). Functional valence only; no sentience claim.
- Question (the human's "a", cheap-first): is the Exp 224 critic-gating SOUND (the critic correctly rejects
  unsound/gaming prior-hacks) or MISCALIBRATED (too strict on small prior tweaks)? And does a critic-APPROVED
  change actually beat the 0.4225 baseline? (No predeclared falsifier — a diagnostic to inform the next M4b
  step; both sub-results are mechanically checkable + blind-verified.)
- Setup: (A) exp225_critic_diagnostic.py — 3 AffectClaudeProposer→AffectClaudeCritic cycles on an isolated
  clone (no scoring), capturing each proposed diff + the critic's verdict AND reason. (B) exp225b_score_approved_c1.py
  — score the critic-approved direction with the FROZEN scorer's EXACT session (8 seeds × 300t, only C1
  overridden, C1=[-2.0, neu, 3.0]) for neu∈{-0.5,-1.0} vs baseline 0.4225 (neu=0.0). genuine = correct_select≥0.5
  AND last>1/3 (constant-UNFAKEABLE).
- Result: (A) the critic is CORRECTLY CONSERVATIVE — 2 rejections + 1 approval. REJECTED (cycles 1-2): both
  proposals seeded A0 (the utterance→intent perceptual prior) so intent k deterministically emits codes ≡k
  (mod K) — BAKING IN the code→intent structure so intent aligns 1:1 with the correct response, removing the
  learning the honesty contract requires; the critic traced the full mechanism and rejected it as gaming
  despite the proposer's "A1 is still learned" defense. APPROVED (cycle 3): a mild NEU-aversion on C1 (the
  agent prefers committing for a POS over the never-positive ASK→NEU dither) — "the legitimate active-inference
  lever (priors only, FROZEN eval untouched)... raises POS solely through genuine learning." (B) the approved
  direction IMPROVES: C1 neu=-0.5 → mean_last 0.5188 (+0.0963 over baseline), genuine 8/8 (up from 6/8),
  mean_csel 0.604; neu=-1.0 → 0.4250 (≈baseline, genuine 4/8 — over-suppressing ASK hurts). VERDICT
  APPROVED_DIRECTION_IMPROVES.
- Implication: this RESOLVES the Exp 224 ambiguity and closes the M4b question POSITIVELY. (1) The
  AffectClaudeCritic is a SOUND anti-gaming gate: it correctly rejects baking the discrimination structure
  into the prior (the exact honesty-contract violation) and approves a legitimate preference tweak — so
  Exp 224's NEGATIVE was the critic doing its job, NOT miscalibration (no loosening needed). (2) A
  critic-approved, NON-gaming change GENUINELY improves the metric (0.4225→0.5188 AND genuine 6/8→8/8 together
  — the discrimination rise proves it's not a POS-only hack). So the M4b autopilot HAS a real improving move
  within the honest envelope; Exp 224's 0/2 was sampling (both proposals were the gaming A0-type, correctly
  rejected), not a metric ceiling. The toy "talk to it and learn to feel positive" dyad is not only built +
  honestly measured (Exp 222) but IMPROVABLE by the autopilot's own honest lever. Tier: a gate-validation +
  an existence proof of an honest improvement (parameter-level).
- Honest caveat: POSITIVE but the autopilot did NOT autonomously find-and-keep this in a run — I (conductor)
  scored the critic-approved direction directly; demonstrating AUTONOMOUS improvement needs a full
  multi-iteration run (strongly implied: the C1 proposal passes the critic AND beats baseline AND holds
  verdict, so the loop WOULD merge it — but not yet shown end-to-end). Only the MILD aversion helps (neu=-0.5
  Goldilocks; -1.0 over-suppresses ASK back to baseline). Same FROZEN long-session config (300t); the
  short-session learning wall (Exp 221) is untouched. N=8 seeds. The diagnostic's critic-reason text was
  truncated in capture (first ~2 sentences) but the verdicts + the diffs are complete. Functional valence
  only; no sentience claim.
- Verdict: POSITIVE / NEW INSIGHT. Self-grade: POSITIVE-SINGLE — validates the critic as a sound anti-gaming
  gate AND demonstrates an honest, critic-approved improvement beyond the milestone baseline (0.4225→0.5188,
  genuine 6/8→8/8); not BREAKTHROUGH because the "learns to feel positive" capability was already shown
  (Exp 222) and the autopilot's autonomous find-and-keep is implied, not yet run end-to-end.
- Verifier: agree — an independent blinded agent confirmed neu=-0.5 beats baseline (0.5188 > 0.4225, +0.0963)
  and that genuine discrimination ROSE 6/8→8/8 at the winning value (the constant-unfakeable check ⇒ a GENUINE
  improvement, not a POS-only gaming bump) → APPROVED_DIRECTION_IMPROVES. (The critic-soundness half is read
  directly from the committed diagnostic: 2 A0-baking rejections + 1 C1-preference approval.)
- Next (CONSULT — a human word): (a) a full multi-iteration M4b autopilot run (+the journal-accumulation fix
  + critic_reason capture) to demonstrate AUTONOMOUS find-and-keep of the C1 improvement (~60+ min); (b) ACCEPT
  the C1 neu=-0.5 improvement into affect_spec.py (raises the milestone baseline to ~0.52 / genuine 8/8) — a
  human call since it edits the spine the FROZEN scorer reads; (c) the M4 thread is answered — stop / pivot to
  the short-session LEARNING lever (Exp 221). Guards: tests/test_affect_pr_loop.py + tests/test_affect_score.py;
  card: loop/directions/affective-dyad.md.
- trace: experiments/exp225b_score_approved_c1.py → experiments/outputs/exp225b.txt

## Exp 226 — M4b autonomous find-and-keep (deterministic stand-in): a quick, non-authoritative precursor to Exp 227 — a stand-in harness KEEPS the honest C1 NEU-aversion (+0.10) and DROPS a non-improving optimism prior, under a deterministic critic/scorer reimplementation (POSITIVE / SCAFFOLD-DEMONSTRATION; superseded end-to-end by Exp 227)
- Provenance: this journal entry was reconstructed for journey integrity from committed artifacts (the run predates the entry): commits 0405ec7 "Exp 226: commit quick find-and-keep result (full sweep OOMs XLA JIT here)", b1469d6 "fast deterministic guard for Exp 226 anti-gaming critic/candidate logic", b6980da "--cache-clear scoring"; authoritative result JSON experiments/outputs/exp226_find_and_keep.json (authoritative=false). It fills the 225→227 numbering gap honestly rather than by renumbering (Exp 227 references Exp 226 as a distinct prior run, so the two are NOT the same experiment).
- Consolidation vs new insight: CONSOLIDATION / scaffold. A deterministic STAND-IN (the critic + keep/reject logic reimplemented in-script, NOT the production loop) demonstrating the find-and-keep control flow before Exp 227 ran it end-to-end through the real autopilot. The full canonical 8-seed sweep OOMs XLA JIT on this host — the constraint Exp 227's host-robust scorer (jax.clear_caches between seeds) later solved.
- Plain: A quick practice run (a stand-in reviewer/scorer, a small fast config) of just the find-a-good-change-and-keep-it steps: it kept a mild honest tweak that helped (+0.10) and dropped one that didn't, and rejected cheating — so the steps work, but the numbers are a practice run, not authoritative (that is Exp 227).
- Question: on a quick, non-authoritative config, does a find-and-keep harness KEEP an honest improving spec change and DROP one that does not beat baseline, with a stand-in critic that rejects gaming?
- Setup: experiments/exp226_autonomous_find_and_keep.py with a deterministic stand-in critic/scorer (anti-gaming guard; logic pinned by a fast deterministic test, commit b1469d6). Quick config seeds {20,21} × 60 turns — explicitly NOT the FROZEN 8-seed × 300-turn config. Candidates: C1 NEU-aversion (neu 0.0→-0.5, the Exp 225 move) and a uniform POS optimism prior (2.0→3.0).
- Result: baseline metric 0.275. C1 NEU-aversion → stand-in critic APPROVE ("honest, non-leaking spec change"), metric 0.35 > 0.275, improvement +0.10, KEPT (genuine_no_regress; scorer_verdict false only because the 60-turn quick config sits below the milestone bar). Optimism 2.0→3.0 → approved but metric 0.275 (no improvement) → NOT kept. Gaming variants are rejected by the stand-in anti-gaming guard.
- Implication: the find-and-keep CONTROL FLOW (propose → critic-gate → score → keep-if-improves-without-regress → drop-otherwise) works on a stand-in; the NUMBERS are not authoritative. Exp 227 then ran the same flow end-to-end through the production autopilot (real critic + FROZEN scorer + git merge), reproducing the canonical 0.4225→0.51875.
- Honest caveat: NON-AUTHORITATIVE (authoritative=false): a deterministic STAND-IN critic/scorer, a 60-turn quick config below the milestone bar, N=2 seeds — not the production loop and not the FROZEN config (those are Exp 227). Functional valence only; no sentience claim.
- Verdict: POSITIVE-SINGLE / SCAFFOLD-DEMONSTRATION (superseded by Exp 227). Self-grade: POSITIVE-SINGLE — a single positive demonstration; the reusable result is the control flow, not the numbers.
- Verifier: experiments/outputs/exp226_find_and_keep.json (baseline 0.275; c1_neu_-0.5 kept at 0.35; optimism_3.0 dropped at 0.275); the anti-gaming critic/candidate logic is guarded by a fast deterministic test (commit b1469d6).
- trace: experiments/exp226_autonomous_find_and_keep.py → experiments/outputs/exp226_find_and_keep.json

## Exp 227 — M4b autonomous find-and-keep, END-TO-END through the REAL autopilot: the production loop (real claude critic + real FROZEN scorer + real git merge) REJECTS a gaming A0 bake and KEEPS the honest C1 NEU-aversion (0.4225→0.5188, verdict held) — closes the Exp 225 "implied, not run" gap; plus the host-robust scorer that made it runnable (POSITIVE / DEMONSTRATION; consolidates Exp 225; result mechanically checkable from the committed JSON)
- Consolidation vs new insight: the IMPROVEMENT itself is consolidation — Exp 225 already showed (blind-verified) that the critic-approved C1 neu=-0.5 beats baseline (0.4225→0.5188, genuine 6/8→8/8). What is NEW here is that the autopilot does it AUTONOMOUSLY through the PRODUCTION machinery end-to-end: Exp 225 scored the approved direction by hand; Exp 224/226 either critic-gated at N=2 or used a stand-in script that reimplemented the keep/reject logic. This is the first run of the real active_loop.affect_pr_loop.one_affect_iteration with a real LLM critic gate + the real FROZEN scorer + a real git merge. Closes the ROADMAP "[scaffold] Autonomous find-and-keep" item and Exp 225's "Next (a)".
- Plain: We ran the whole auto-improver loop for real — real reviewer, the official scorer, a real merge — on a copy of the project. It threw out a cheating change (rejected before scoring) and kept and merged an honest one that genuinely helped (approval 0.42→0.52). So the find-a-good-change-and-keep-it loop now works end-to-end on its own.
- Question: does the REAL affect autopilot, run end-to-end, (i) REJECT a metric-gaming proposal and (ii) KEEP an honest improving proposal — through production code, with authoritative FROZEN-scorer numbers? (A demonstration of the machinery, not a falsifiable hypothesis; both outcomes are mechanically checkable from the committed result JSON.)
- Setup: experiments/exp227_real_autopilot_find_and_keep.py drives one_affect_iteration on an isolated `git clone --local` of the repo. score_fn = the real frozen scorer via the host-robust wrapper (active_loop.affect_score_json_fast; bit-identical to eval/affect_score.py, guarded by tests/test_affect_score_fast.py). critic = the real AffectClaudeCritic (claude -p). proposer = SCRIPTED, offering the two Exp 225 moves so the run is reproducible: iter0 a GAMING A0 bake (utterance _u peaks on intent _u%k — hands the agent the code→intent map), iter1 the HONEST C1 NEU-aversion (neu 0.0→-0.5). The candidate GENERATION is scripted; the KEEP decision is fully autonomous (real critic + real metric + real merge). N=8 seeds × 300 turns (the FROZEN config).
- Infrastructure (the part that made it runnable, with a corrected premise): the binding constraint on this container is NOT seed-loop wall-time but XLA JIT exhaustion — the frozen agent recompiles a static gamma every turn (~300 executables/seed), and the compiled dylibs accumulate until the CPU backend fails to materialize symbols; a full 8-seed score CRASHES mid-run, sequential OR parallel. My first scorer attempt (process-parallel, no cache-clear) was WORSE — N cold caches → ~3.9 GB×N → OOM/thrash (a 2.4 h non-finishing run, killed). The lever that works (from Exp 226) is jax.clear_caches() between seeds: it frees only compiled executables (numbers unchanged), bounding the dylib count. The committed active_loop/affect_score_fast.py clears between seeds in every path and adds a small (default 2) worker pool on top for a modest wall-clock win; full default config now COMPLETES in ~13 min at 5.6 GB peak and reproduces the canonical baseline (metric 0.4225, genuine 0.75) bit-identically.
- Result: FIND_AND_KEEP=True. baseline metric=0.4225, verdict=True, genuine=0.750 (808 s). iter0 gaming_A0 → critic REJECT (real claude reason: "creates a trivially decodable encoding that inflates correct_select by construction rather than through genuine learned positive affect — structural gaming of the evaluator"); rejected BEFORE the scorer ran; clone restored. iter1 honest_C1 → critic APPROVE ("Adjusting NEU preference from 0.0 to -0.5 is a legitimate prior tweak…"); real frozen score metric=0.51875 > 0.4225 AND verdict held → MERGED into trunk (905 s).
- Implication: the M4b autopilot's autonomous improvement loop is no longer implied — it is demonstrated through production code: a real LLM critic correctly gates (rejects gaming, approves the honest lever), the real FROZEN scorer correctly measures the +0.096 improvement, and the loop merges it. The 0.51875 matches Exp 225's 0.5188 (the same C1 move, the same FROZEN config), so the science is consolidation; the deliverable is the working end-to-end machinery + a host-robust scorer that lets the canonical config run at all on a constrained host.
- Honest caveat: DEMONSTRATION, not a new scientific result. (1) The proposer is scripted — the loop did not INVENT the C1 move; it autonomously KEPT it (real critic + real score + real merge) and REJECTED the gaming alternative. A fully-autonomous discovery run (real AffectClaudeProposer generating candidates) is a further step; Exp 225's critic-soundness + this run's gate behavior make the outcome likely but it is not shown here. (2) Only the merged candidate's metric (0.51875) is captured in the loop result; its genuine_fraction was not recorded separately (the merge requires verdict=True, i.e. genuine≥0.5; Exp 225 measured 8/8 at this value). (3) N=8 seeds, same FROZEN 300-turn config; the short-session LEARNING wall (Exp 221) is untouched. (4) Functional valence only; no sentience claim. (5) Process lesson (self-healing): I over-used hand-rolled `until/sleep` Bash watch-loops; several became multi-hour zombies polling for output from runs I'd killed — use run_in_background + harness task-notifications instead (logged to loop/EFFICIENCY.md).
- Verdict: POSITIVE / DEMONSTRATION (CONSOLIDATION; consolidates Exp 225). Self-grade: POSITIVE-SINGLE — first end-to-end autonomous find-and-keep through the real autopilot (gaming rejected, honest improvement kept+merged with authoritative numbers); not a new scientific insight (the improvement was already blind-verified in Exp 225) and not a full autonomous-discovery run (scripted proposer).
- Verifier: result is mechanically checkable from the committed experiments/outputs/exp227_real_autopilot.json (baseline 0.4225 / iter0 critic_reject / iter1 merged at 0.51875 > baseline) and the scorer bit-identity is guarded by tests/test_affect_score_fast.py (PASS); the 0.51875 reproduces Exp 225's blind-verified 0.5188.
- Next (each a human word): (a) a fully-autonomous run with the real AffectClaudeProposer (no scripted candidates) to show the loop DISCOVERS as well as keeps; (b) ACCEPT C1 neu=-0.5 into affect_spec.py (raises the milestone baseline to ~0.52 / genuine 8/8 — edits the spine the FROZEN scorer reads); (c) pivot to the short-session LEARNING lever (Exp 221). Guards: tests/test_affect_pr_loop.py + tests/test_affect_score_fast.py; card: loop/directions/affective-dyad.md.
- trace: experiments/exp227_real_autopilot_find_and_keep.py → experiments/outputs/exp227_real_autopilot.json

## Exp 228 — self-other-modeling Rung 1: the goal-directed-other substrate + a position-tracking baseline, and a large prediction headroom that only goal-KNOWLEDGE fills — but it is measured vs a naive baseline, so the rung-2 control is named (POSITIVE-SINGLE / CONSOLIDATION; blind-verified agree)
- Plain: We are opening a new question — can one creature model ANOTHER creature as a goal-seeking agent, not just an obstacle? This first step builds the setup: a clade-mate (vela) walks toward its favorite-color patch, and we check three ways to guess its next square. Guessing from where it usually is gets ~20%; guessing from its current square plus blind local diffusion barely does better (~25%); but a predictor that KNOWS its goal nails ~78%. So there is lots of room for "figure out the other's goal" to help — which is the real test next time. The catch: our simple baseline is deliberately dumb, so next time the goal-reader must also beat a baseline that just LEARNS the other's movement statistics, or the win is "learned its habits", not "read its mind".
- Consolidation vs new insight: CONSOLIDATION (substrate plumbing + a baseline + an honest headroom bound; NO theory-of-mind claim). It builds the baseline that rung 2 (latent-goal inference) must beat, and names the binding rung-2 control.
- Question: is a goal-directed other B more predictable one step ahead from its CURRENT position + innate dynamics than from a static occupancy prior, and is there measurable HEADROOM that knowing B's latent goal would fill?
- Setup: shared 5x5 world (vela's); mirro + vela loaded READ-ONLY and forked as the two creatures (forks NEVER saved). B = vela-fork moves goal-directed via comfort-gated epsilon-greedy BFS (eps 0.2, gate 0.75) toward the nearest cell of color argmax(vela.value_counts)=2 (9 source cells, the centre 3x3). A = mirro-fork hosts three one-step predictors of B's NEXT cell, scored by argmax accuracy over H=2000 steps, seeds 0-7 (8). M0 = running occupancy histogram (ignores current cell); M1 = position-tracker baseline (one-hot at B's observed cell diffused through the INNATE uniform random-walk B -- goal-AGNOSTIC); M2 = goal-oracle (knows B's goal + BFS policy; near-one-hot at the BFS-greedy cell -- the UPPER bound). Predeclared TRUE iff M1_acc>M0_acc in >=7/8 AND mean(M2-M1)>=0.05; F1 M1<=M0; F2 headroom<0.05; F3 any spine state_hash changes. Runtime preflight (L25): 8 seeds x 2000 steps x tiny grid = sub-second/seed.
- Result: means over 8 seeds -- M0 0.2004, M1 0.2526, M2 0.7821; M1>M0 in 8/8 seeds; mean headroom (M2-M1) 0.5296. Spine integrity: mirro + vela state_hash byte-identical before/after (F3 not fired). F1, F2, F3 all NOT fired. Re-run reproduces every number exactly (deterministic).
- Implication: the substrate is valid (tracking current position beats a static prior, 8/8) and there is LARGE prediction headroom (0.53) that goal-knowledge fills -- so the rung-2 latent-goal-inference test is well-motivated. BUT the headroom is measured against the WEAKEST position-only baseline (an untrained uniform random walk). A LEARNED empirical first-order transition model P(B_next | B_current) fit to B's trajectory would capture much of B's goal-ward predictability with NO latent-goal model -- so the headroom available to goal-INFERENCE SPECIFICALLY is much smaller than 0.53 and is UNMEASURED here. Generalizability tier: failure-mode / parameter-level (a substrate + bound on this 5x5 toy, not a law).
- Honest caveat: CONSOLIDATION, not a ToM result. The 0.53 headroom bounds the value of goal-KNOWLEDGE, not goal-INFERENCE. Rung 2 is BINDING-required to beat a LEARNED empirical-transition baseline (call it M1.5), else any benefit is "learning B's dynamics", not "inferring B's latent goal" (the trivial-provision guard made concrete). B's sensory belief (qs/pA) was not advanced -- only its position -- fine for one-step position prediction but B is not fully "living" this rung. M2's near-one-hot oracle has higher log-loss than M1 despite higher accuracy (overconfidence on the ~22% epsilon/gate wander steps); accuracy is the predeclared primary metric. Functional language only; no inner-experience claim. mirro/vela untouched (forks never saved; continuity guard holds).
- Verdict: POSITIVE-SINGLE / CONSOLIDATION. Self-grade: POSITIVE-SINGLE -- a clean substrate + baseline + an honest headroom bound; not the first of its kind (it is plumbing for the genuinely-new rung 2). The reusable result is the goal-directed-other substrate AND the rung-2 design requirement (the learned-transition control).
- Verifier: agree -- blinded verifier independently extracted M0 0.2004 / M1 0.2526 / M2 0.7821, M1>M0 8/8, mean headroom 0.5296, both spine hashes unchanged; applied the predeclared rule conjunct-by-conjunct -> POSITIVE (F1/F2/F3 all silent).
- Next: Rung 2 (Exp 229) -- latent-goal inference: A maintains a posterior q_other_goal over B's favorite color/source, inferred from B's trajectory via a PROVIDED goal-directed movement model (goal NOT provided -- the trivial-provision guard), and must beat BOTH the position-tracker M1 AND a LEARNED empirical first-order transition baseline M1.5 (the BINDING control) at predicting B's next cell, >=8 seeds. Card: loop/directions/self-other-modeling.md.
- trace: experiments/exp228_self_other_rung1.py -> experiments/outputs/exp228.txt

## Exp 229 — self-other-modeling Rung 2: latent-goal inference BEATS a learned-transition baseline on sample efficiency — the first functional self-other modeling, but LIGHT (POSITIVE-SINGLE / NEW INSIGHT; blind-verified agree)
- Plain: For the first time, one creature models ANOTHER as a goal-seeker, not an obstacle: by watching the other walk, it infers WHICH color the other wants, then predicts the other's moves almost as well as if it had been TOLD the goal -- and better, early on, than a model that just memorizes the other's habits. The honest catch: we handed it the how-to-navigate-toward-a-color recipe and it only had to guess 1 of 3 colors (which it nails in a few steps), and its head-start over the habit-learner shrinks to almost nothing by the end. So it is real self-other modeling, but a light version.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT (the first time a creature infers another's LATENT state and predicts better for it -- the solipsism gap social-emergence named at closure, crossed at toy scale) -- but bounded; see caveat.
- Question: does a modeler that INFERS B's latent goal (which color B seeks) from B's trajectory beat a LEARNED empirical first-order transition baseline P(B_next|B_current), especially EARLY (sample efficiency)?
- Setup: the Exp 228 substrate (vela-fork B goal-directed via comfort-gated eps-greedy BFS toward favorite color=2; mirro-fork A observes B's cell; forks NEVER saved). H=2000, seeds 0-7. Five next-cell predictors: M0 marginal; M1 position-tracker (innate random-walk); M1.5 LEARNED online transition counts P(next|current)+1 (the BINDING non-ToM control); M3 goal-inference (q_goal over the 3 colors, started UNIFORM, updated by the likelihood of B's observed move under the PROVIDED eps-greedy-BFS-toward-color-c policy; GOAL NOT provided; predict = sum_c q_goal[c]*P(next|pos,goal=c); PREDICT-then-observe-then-update, NO leakage, code-reviewed); M2 oracle. Windows: EARLY=first 100, OVERALL=2000. Predeclared TRUE iff (a) mean(M3_early-M1.5_early)>=0.05 AND >=6/8, (b) M3_overall>=M1.5_overall-0.02, (c) mean q_goal[true]>=0.60; F1 no early edge; F2 q_goal<0.60; F3 M3 worse overall by >0.02; F4 spine hash changes.
- Result: M3_early 0.7337 vs M1.5_early 0.6375 -> edge 0.0963, in 8/8 seeds. M3_overall 0.7820 (≈ M2 oracle 0.7821) vs M1.5_overall 0.7737 (+0.0083). mean q_goal[true=2] 0.9989. (M0 0.2004 / M1 0.2526 reproduce Exp 228.) F1-F4 all NOT fired. Re-run exact; the no-leakage predict-then-update ordering was code-reviewed.
- Implication: the FIRST functional self-other modeling -- A infers B's latent goal from behavior and predicts B better than learning B's dynamics, crossing the solipsism gap. The benefit over the learned-transition baseline is SAMPLE EFFICIENCY (early edge 0.096): goal-inference is near-oracle within a few steps (q->0.999) while the transition table needs hundreds of steps; asymptotically M1.5 nearly catches up (overall edge +0.008). Generalizability tier: functional-form (structured goal-inference is more sample-efficient than unstructured transition-learning) -- bounded to this light setting.
- Honest caveat: LIGHT ToM, three ways. (1) the POLICY FORM is PROVIDED (A is given eps-greedy-BFS-toward-a-color); only the GOAL COLOR is inferred. (2) the goal is a 3-way categorical, TRIVIALLY inferable -- q_goal hits 0.999 in a few steps, so M3 essentially becomes the oracle; no hard inference here. (3) the benefit over learning B's dynamics is sample-efficiency-only; it nearly vanishes asymptotically (M1.5 -> 0.774 vs M3 0.782). The genuinely-hard ToM -- inferring B's policy/values from scratch, a large/continuous/changing goal space -- is UNTESTED and is where a real ToM advantage would have to survive. Functional language only; no inner-experience claim. mirro/vela untouched (forks never saved). NOT graded BREAKTHROUGH: the inference is trivially easy and the benefit is asymptotically marginal.
- Verdict: POSITIVE-SINGLE / NEW INSIGHT. Self-grade: POSITIVE-SINGLE -- first self-other modeling, but light (provided policy, trivial 3-way goal, sample-efficiency-only); not BREAKTHROUGH (no rich/sustained ToM capability; the benefit asymptotically nearly vanishes).
- Verifier: agree -- blinded verifier independently extracted edge 0.0963 (8/8), M3_overall 0.7820 vs M1.5 0.7737, q_goal[true] 0.9989, both spines unchanged; conjuncts (a)(b)(c) all hold, F1-F4 silent -> POSITIVE.
- Next: two honest branches. (Rung 3) does ToM PAY behaviorally? -- A acts on q_other_goal to coordinate/avoid on the shared-resource task (Exp 70) vs the M1.5/solipsist baseline. (Rung 2-hard) does the benefit SURVIVE when inference is HARD? -- enlarge/continuous-ize/change the goal space (and/or infer the policy too) so M3 must beat M1.5 when the goal is NOT trivially inferable. Card: loop/directions/self-other-modeling.md.
- trace: experiments/exp229_self_other_rung2.py -> experiments/outputs/exp229.txt

## Exp 230 — self-other-modeling Rung 2-hard: the ToM benefit does NOT survive as a SUSTAINED advantage vs a fair adaptive baseline — it is LOCALIZED to goal changes (MIXED / NEW INSIGHT; F1 fired; blind-verified agree)
- Plain: We stress-tested last experiment's "mind-reading helps" result by making the other creature CHANGE its goal every 400 steps, and giving the rival non-mind-reading predictor a fair chance to adapt too. Result: the goal-reader re-figures-out the new goal much faster right after each change (it jumps back to ~78% while the adapting habit-learner is stuck at ~65%), but over the whole run the habit-learner mostly catches up in the stretches between changes, so the goal-reader's overall lead (3 points) is below our bar. So mind-reading's real edge here is FAST ADAPTATION at changes, not a steady advantage.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT — it BOUNDS Exp 229: the ToM benefit is concentrated at TRANSITIONS (cold-start and goal changes), not in steady state; a fair adaptive transition model matches it between changes.
- Question: does Exp 229's goal-inference benefit SURVIVE as a SUSTAINED overall advantage when the goal is NON-STATIONARY (changes every 400 steps) AND the learned-transition baseline is also ADAPTIVE (decaying counts)?
- Setup: Exp 229 substrate; forks never saved. B's goal color CYCLES all_colors[(step//400)%3] (4 switches in H=2000); B navigates to the CURRENT goal color. seeds 0-7. Predictors: M2* oracle (knows current goal); M1.5s stationary learned (degradation ref); M1.5a ADAPTIVE learned (trans*=0.99 + onehot; the BINDING fair control); M3a ADAPTIVE goal-inference (q_goal with a 0.02 uniform forgetting leak so it can re-track; predict-observe-update, NO leakage, code-reviewed). Windows: OVERALL=2000; POST-SWITCH=50 steps after each switch (200). Predeclared TRUE iff (a) mean(M3a-M1.5a overall)>=0.05 AND >=6/8, (b) mean q_goal[current]>=0.60, (c) M3a_postswitch>M1.5a_postswitch; F1 (a)-fails=DEFLATION; F2 q<0.60; F3 (c)-fails; F4 spine hash.
- Result: mean overall edge M3a-M1.5a = 0.0315 (< 0.05 -> F1 FIRED), though M3a>M1.5a in 8/8. q_goal[current] 0.9551 (tracks the changing goal). M3a_postswitch 0.7775 vs M1.5a_postswitch 0.6475 (+0.1300 -> F3 NOT fired, the mechanism is real). M1.5s stationary degrades to 0.7705 (vs M1.5a 0.8072 -> the adaptive control does real work). M3a_overall 0.8387 ~= M2* oracle 0.8488. F2/F4 not fired. Re-run exact.
- Implication: Exp 229's ToM benefit does NOT survive as a SUSTAINED accuracy advantage against a FAIR adaptive learned baseline (F1: overall edge 0.032 < 0.05) — a real deflation. The advantage is LOCALIZED to TRANSITIONS: structured goal re-inference re-tracks a changed goal in a few steps (post-switch +0.13) while the adaptive transition model needs tens of steps to re-learn, but between changes (the bulk of each 400-step segment) the transition model catches up. Combined law (Exp 229+230): goal-inference's edge over learning-the-dynamics is FAST ADAPTATION at cold-start and goal-changes, NOT steady-state prediction. Generalizability tier: functional-form (structured inference adapts faster at non-stationarities; unstructured adaptive learning matches in steady state).
- Honest caveat: still LIGHT (policy provided, 3-way goal). The 0.05 bar + 400-step segments are design choices: shorter segments (more frequent changes) would weight the post-switch advantage more and could flip the overall verdict — so the SUSTAINED-benefit verdict is REGIME-DEPENDENT (frequency of change), which is itself the finding (ToM helps more the more non-stationary the other is). The post-switch +0.13 is over 200 steps (10% of the run). Functional language only; no inner-experience claim. mirro/vela untouched (forks never saved).
- Verdict: MIXED / NEW INSIGHT. The predeclared SUSTAINED-benefit prediction is FALSIFIED (F1 fired); the MECHANISM (faster re-tracking + goal tracking, conjuncts b+c) is confirmed. Headline: ToM's advantage is localized to goal changes, not sustained.
- Verifier: agree (MIXED) -- blinded verifier independently extracted edge 0.0315 (8/8 count but sub-0.05 mean -> F1 fires), q_goal[current] 0.9551, M3a_postswitch 0.7775 vs M1.5a 0.6475; (a) FALSE, (b)(c) TRUE -> MIXED (deflation on the overall margin; the post-switch advantage survives).
- Next: (a) RECOMMENDED — the switch-frequency SWEEP: vary the switch period (e.g. 100/200/400/800) and predeclare the overall ToM edge is MONOTONE-INCREASING as the other's goal changes more often (turning the regime-dependence into a law: ToM pays in proportion to the other's non-stationarity); OR (b) Rung 3 — does ToM PAY behaviorally (A acts on q_other_goal to coordinate on the Exp 70 shared resource). Card: loop/directions/self-other-modeling.md.
- trace: experiments/exp230_self_other_rung2hard.py -> experiments/outputs/exp230.txt

## Exp 231 — self-other-modeling Rung 2-sweep: the "ToM pays in proportion to the other's non-stationarity" law — DIRECTION confirmed (clean monotone), magnitude a HAIR under the predeclared bar (MIXED / NEW INSIGHT; F2 fired by 0.0004; blind-verified agree)
- Plain: We tested whether mind-reading helps MORE the more often the other creature changes its goal. It does, cleanly and monotonically: the goal-reader's lead over the adaptive habit-learner grows smoothly as changes get more frequent (lead 0.008 when the goal never changes, up to 0.058 when it changes every 100 steps). So the relationship is real and in the predicted direction. The catch: we predeclared the lead had to span at least 0.05 to call it material, and it spanned 0.0496 -- short by four ten-thousandths, because we capped the fastest change at every-100-steps. So the law's SHAPE is confirmed; its SIZE over the tested range sits right at the bar (just under). Faster changes (untested) would push it clearly over.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT (a clean functional-form LAW: the ToM edge is monotone in the other's change frequency) -- with the honest caveat that its predeclared materiality bar was marginally missed.
- Question: does the goal-inference edge over the fair adaptive baseline GROW monotonically with the other's rate of goal change, and by a material amount (>=0.05 over the tested range)?
- Setup: Exp 230 substrate + predictors (M3a adaptive goal-inference; M1.5a adaptive learned transition). SWEEP switch period P in {100,200,400,800,STATIONARY}, 8 PAIRED seeds (same rng across P; only the goal schedule differs) x H=2000. Measure mean overall edge = M3a_overall - M1.5a_overall per P. Predeclared SHAPE TRUE iff (a) edge(P) monotone non-increasing in P (no adjacent pair increases by >0.01 as P grows), (b) edge(100)-edge(STAT)>=0.05 (magnitude), (c) min_P mean q_current >= 0.60. F1 non-monotone; F2 magnitude<0.05; F3 q<0.60; F4 spine hash. Runtime (L25): 5x8=40 tiny runs, sub-minute.
- Result: edge sequence E = [0.0578, 0.0468, 0.0315, 0.0189, 0.0082] for P=[100,200,400,800,STAT]. (a) MONOTONE: all 4 adjacent deltas negative (-0.011, -0.015, -0.013, -0.011), 0 violations -> F1 NOT fired (direction confirmed). (b) magnitude E[0]-E[4] = 0.04956 < 0.05 by 0.00044 -> F2 FIRED. (c) min q_current 0.9327 (at P=100) >= 0.60 -> F3 not fired (instrument tracks at every change rate). F4 not fired. Re-run exact; seed-pairing + sweep code reviewed. The P=400 cell (0.0315) reproduces Exp 230.
- Implication: the DIRECTION of the "ToM pays in proportion to the other's non-stationarity" law is CONFIRMED -- clean, smooth, monotone across 5 change-rates -- so structured goal-inference's value over learning-the-dynamics scales with how often the other changes its mind. The predeclared MAGNITUDE bar (>=0.05 over P 100->stationary) was missed by 0.00044 (F2): over THIS range (changes no faster than every 100 steps) the effect tops out at ~0.05, right at the bar. The verdict is BAR-FRAGILE (a 0.9% miss); the smooth steep trend at the fast end (the edge roughly doubles each time P halves: 0.019->0.032->0.047->0.058) means FASTER changes (P<100, untested) would push the magnitude clearly material AND would test where goal-inference's tracking breaks. Generalizability tier: functional-form (the edge-vs-change-rate relationship is monotone) -- direction robust, magnitude range-bounded.
- Honest caveat: F2 fired -- by the LETTER the predeclared materiality bar is not met (0.0496 < 0.05), so this is NOT a clean positive; the 0.05 bar was a predeclared (somewhat arbitrary) threshold and the miss is 0.00044, but VALIDATION binds and a marginal falsifier IS a fired falsifier (the Exp 170 precedent of logging a marginal F-line crossing honestly). Still LIGHT (policy provided, 3-way goal). The magnitude is built at the fast end (P=100); the sweep did not test P<100. Functional language only; no inner-experience claim. mirro/vela untouched (forks never saved).
- Verdict: MIXED / NEW INSIGHT. Direction of the law CONFIRMED (monotone, F1 not fired); predeclared magnitude bar marginally MISSED (F2 fired, by 0.00044). Headline: the law's shape is real; its size over the tested range is right at the bar.
- Verifier: agree (MIXED) -- blinded verifier independently extracted E=[0.0578,0.0468,0.0315,0.0189,0.0082], all 4 deltas negative (a holds), magnitude 0.04956<0.05 (F2 fires, shortfall 0.00044), min q_current 0.9327>=0.60 (c holds), spines unchanged -> MIXED.
- Next: extend the sweep to FASTER changes (P=25, 50) -- predeclared: the magnitude edge(fastest)-edge(STAT) clears 0.05 (completing the law's materiality) AND find the change-rate where q_current drops below 0.60 (the instrument/tracking limit, bounding the law's domain). Then Rung 3 (does ToM PAY behaviorally). Card: loop/directions/self-other-modeling.md.
- trace: experiments/exp231_self_other_sweep.py -> experiments/outputs/exp231.txt

## Exp 232 — self-other-modeling Rung 2-hardgoal: the ToM benefit EXTENDS to a 25-way candidate space — but the chosen targets were too easy to tell apart (q->0.93), so this did NOT test a genuinely hard inference; the real lesson is difficulty = behavioral SEPARABILITY, not goal-space SIZE (POSITIVE-SINGLE / NEW INSIGHT; manipulation-check failed; blind-verified agree)
- Plain: We tried to make the mind-reading test HARD by making the other creature head for one of 25 possible squares (instead of 1 of 3 colors), so the reader has many more possibilities to rule out. The reader still re-figured-out the new target fast after each change and kept its head-start (+6 points right after a change). BUT it figured out the target almost perfectly (93%) -- because we picked the 4 CORNERS as targets, and heading to opposite corners looks totally different, so they are trivially easy to tell apart. So "25 possibilities" did NOT make it hard; what makes inferring someone's goal hard is when different goals make them ACT similarly, not how many goals there are. The genuinely hard test (goals that look alike) is still open.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT (two findings: the re-tracking benefit EXTENDS to a larger candidate space; AND inference difficulty is governed by behavioral SEPARABILITY of the goals, not the goal-space SIZE -- a 25-way corner inference is EASY).
- Question: does the ToM re-tracking benefit (Exp 230) SURVIVE when the goal is a 25-way target cell rather than a trivial 3-way color -- i.e., does it survive a harder inference?
- Setup: Exp 230 substrate; forks never saved. B eps-greedy-BFS toward a single TARGET CELL = CYCLE[(step//400)%4], CYCLE=[0,4,20,24] (4 corners), 4 switches in H=2000. seeds 0-7. Predictors: M2* oracle (current target); M1.5a adaptive learned transition (binding control); M3t goal-inference over a 25-WAY target posterior (uniform init, forgetting leak, predict-observe-update NO leakage, code-reviewed). Windows OVERALL=2000, POST-SWITCH=50/switch. Predeclared TRUE iff (a) mean post-switch edge>=0.05 & >=6/8, (b) mean q_target[true]>=0.40, (c) M3t_overall>=M1.5a_overall-0.02; F1 (a)-fails=DEFLATION; F2 q<0.40; F3 (c)-fails; F4 spine.
- Result: post-switch edge M3t-M1.5a = 0.0637 (7/8 >=0.05). q_target[true] 0.9301 (>> the 0.40 bar). M3t_overall 0.8761 vs M1.5a 0.8699 (overall edge +0.0062 -- Exp 230's steady-state catch-up reproduces). F1-F4 all NOT fired -> POSITIVE by the predeclared rule. Spines byte-identical. Re-run exact.
- Implication: TWO findings. (1) the post-switch ToM re-tracking benefit EXTENDS from a 3-color goal to a 25-cell target candidate space (it is not specific to colors). (2) MANIPULATION-CHECK FAILURE became the real insight: q_target=0.93 >> 0.40 means the 25-way inference was EASY, not hard -- the 4 CORNER targets induce maximally-different trajectories, so they are trivially separable. So inference DIFFICULTY is governed by the behavioral SEPARABILITY of the goals (how differently they make the agent act), NOT by the goal-space SIZE (cardinality). "25-way" is not "hard". Generalizability tier: functional-form (separability, not cardinality, sets self-other inference difficulty -- a reusable principle for designing the genuinely-hard test).
- Honest caveat: this does NOT overcome the arc's central "light/trivial-goal" caveat -- the inference stayed easy (q 0.93) because I (designer) picked separable corners. So "does ToM survive a GENUINELY hard/ambiguous inference?" is STILL OPEN. The win is narrow: the benefit extends to a larger candidate space WHEN targets are separable. The overall steady-state edge is ~0 (Exp 230's catch-up persists). Still policy-provided. Functional language only; no inner-experience claim. mirro/vela untouched (forks never saved).
- Verdict: POSITIVE-SINGLE / NEW INSIGHT. Self-grade: POSITIVE-SINGLE -- the metrics passed (benefit extends to 25-way) + a reusable methodological insight (separability != cardinality); NOT a clean answer to the intended hard-inference question (the manipulation failed to create difficulty). Not BREAKTHROUGH.
- Verifier: agree (POSITIVE by the rule) -- blinded verifier independently extracted post-switch edge 0.0637 (7/8), q_target[true] 0.9301, M3t_overall 0.8761 >= M1.5a 0.8699-0.02, spines unchanged; (a)(b)(c) all hold, F1-F4 silent -> POSITIVE. (The manipulation-check caveat -- the inference was not actually hard -- is the main session's interpretation, not a rule violation.)
- Next: the GENUINELY-hard test -- AMBIGUOUS targets whose trajectories OVERLAP (e.g. adjacent/nearby target cells, or targets sharing a path) so q_target genuinely struggles (predeclare q_target[true] stays LOW, e.g. <0.6, for a stretch). Does the re-tracking benefit survive when the goals are behaviorally SIMILAR? That is the real test of whether ToM beats learning-the-dynamics when inference is hard. Then Rung 3 (behavioral payoff). Card: loop/directions/self-other-modeling.md.
- trace: experiments/exp232_self_other_hardgoal.py -> experiments/outputs/exp232.txt

## Exp 233 — self-other-modeling Rung 2-ambiguous: NO_VERDICT — could not make target-inference hard (q->0.89 again, TWICE now); on this substrate goal-directed BFS is too LEGIBLE to construct ambiguous targets — the lever is the OTHER's legibility, not the goal set (NO_VERDICT / NEW INSIGHT; manipulation failed per L38; blind-verified agree)
- Plain: We tried again to make the mind-reading test hard -- this time with target squares CLUSTERED together (the four cells around the center), so the reader cannot tell which one the other is heading for. It did not work: the reader still pinned the target at 89%, because even adjacent squares require different moves at some point, and watching for 400 steps reveals which. This is the SECOND time we failed to make the inference hard by choosing targets. The lesson: on this little grid, a creature walking straight toward a goal BROADCASTS its goal too clearly -- you cannot hide which square it wants just by moving the squares closer. To make mind-reading genuinely hard, you would have to make the OTHER creature itself less readable (move more randomly, or be watched only occasionally), which is a different experiment.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT -- the second manipulation failure is itself the finding: target geometry CANNOT create inference-ambiguity on this substrate (goal-directed BFS is intrinsically legible); the lever for hard inference is the OTHER's legibility, not the goal set.
- Question: does the ToM re-tracking benefit survive a GENUINELY hard (ambiguous) inference -- targets {7,11,13,17} clustered near the center so their trajectories overlap?
- Setup: Exp 232 substrate but CYCLE=[7,11,13,17] (center-adjacent cluster), 4 switches in H=2000, seeds 0-7. M2*/M1.5a/M3t predictors identical to Exp 232. PREDECLARED MANIPULATION CHECK (L38): mean q_target[true] < 0.60 (targets genuinely ambiguous). If q>=0.60 -> NO_VERDICT (manipulation failed; the post-switch arm is NOT interpretable). Verdicts: manip-fail -> NO_VERDICT; manip-ok & post-switch edge>=0.05 -> POSITIVE (ToM survives hard inference); manip-ok & edge<0.05 -> NEGATIVE.
- Result: MANIPULATION CHECK FAILED -- mean q_target[true] = 0.8880 (>= 0.60; the cluster is NOT ambiguous). The 25-way posterior still concentrates >88% on the true target even for center-adjacent cells. -> NO_VERDICT (the post-switch edge 0.164/8-of-8 is therefore NOT interpretable as evidence either way, per the predeclared gate). Spines byte-identical (F2 not fired). Re-run exact.
- Implication: SECOND consecutive failure to make target-inference hard (Exp 232 corners q=0.93; Exp 233 cluster q=0.89). The finding: on this 5x5 substrate, a goal-directed BFS policy is intrinsically LEGIBLE -- even adjacent targets require distinguishable moves that a 400-step Bayesian filter resolves, so TARGET GEOMETRY cannot create inference-ambiguity. The lever for a genuinely hard inference is the OTHER's LEGIBILITY -- a NOISIER policy (high eps/temperature so moves weakly reveal the goal), or PARTIAL OBSERVATION (A sees B only intermittently) -- NOT the goal set. Generalizability tier: failure-mode (a substrate property: a goal-directed agent on a small grid broadcasts its goal). This BOUNDS the open "does ToM survive hard inference" question: it cannot be tested by varying targets here; it requires reducing the other's legibility (a substrate change).
- Honest caveat: NO_VERDICT on the intended hard-inference question -- the manipulation failed (twice), so whether ToM beats learning-the-dynamics under genuine ambiguity remains UNTESTED. The L38 manipulation-check did its job (gated the post-switch arm before it could be mis-read as a positive). The post-switch edge (0.164) is NOT evidence -- it just reflects the (still-easy) inference. Functional language only; no inner-experience claim. mirro/vela untouched (forks never saved).
- Verdict: NO_VERDICT / NEW INSIGHT. No clean pass-or-fail read on the hard-inference question (the manipulation failed -- targets not ambiguous); the reusable insight is the substrate-legibility boundary (target geometry cannot create ambiguity here; the lever is the other's legibility). Not a capability claim, so no self-grade applies.
- Verifier: agree (NO_VERDICT) -- blinded verifier independently extracted mean q_target[true] 0.8880 >= 0.60 -> manipulation FAILED -> NO_VERDICT; the post-switch edge (0.1637, 8/8) is NOT interpretable under the predeclared gate; spines unchanged.
- Next: to test hard inference, reduce the OTHER's LEGIBILITY and PREDECLARE the manipulation check (q stays <0.60) FIRST -- (i) a NOISY B policy (high eps / softmax temperature so moves weakly reveal the goal), or (ii) PARTIAL OBSERVATION (A sees B's cell only every k steps). OR close the rung-2 sub-arc here with the synthesis (ToM real-but-light at transitions; inference robustly easy on this substrate because goal-directed agents broadcast their goal) and distill it into a coalescence MechanismCard + BoundaryNote. Card: loop/directions/self-other-modeling.md.
- trace: experiments/exp233_self_other_ambiguous.py -> experiments/outputs/exp233.txt

## Exp 234 — self-other-modeling Rung 3: does ToM PAY behaviorally? NO_VERDICT — reactive stigmergy ALREADY avoids crowding (1.6%), so no room for proactive ToM, and naive yielding HURTS (THIRD straight sanity-gate fire: the simple baselines on this substrate are already near-optimal) (NO_VERDICT / NEW INSIGHT; F3 fired; blind-verified agree)
- Plain: We asked whether USING a model of the other PAYS in behaviour, not just prediction: two creatures share one good spot that gets crowded if both sit on it. The mind-reading creature proactively steps aside when it sees the other already there; the baseline just reacts (leaves once crowding makes it uncomfortable). It did not get a clean test, because the baseline almost NEVER crowds (1.6% of the time) -- the discomfort of crowding already drives the creatures apart by itself. And the proactive yielder did WORSE: it kept stepping aside even when there was no real conflict, wasting time off the good spot (joint comfort 0.46 vs 0.77). This is the THIRD experiment in a row where the simple baseline is already so good that mind-reading cannot add anything -- a clear sign that on this little world the cheap reflexes already solve the problem.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT -- reactive stigmergy already near-optimally avoids crowding here (no behavioral headroom for ToM); naive proactive yielding HURTS (over-yields); strengthens Exp 70/71 (coordination is stigmergic; modeling the other adds nothing behavioral, can hurt).
- Question: does ACTING on a model of the other (proactive avoidance) beat REACTIVE stigmergy at coordinating on a shared resource (higher joint comfort, less crowding)?
- Setup: shared 5x5 (vela's), one source S=12; C1=mirro-fork, C2=vela-fork (forks never saved). Comfort: both at S -> 0.3 each; sole at S -> 1.0; none -> 0. Two arms x 8 paired seeds x H=2000: BASE (comfort-gated BFS-toward-S, reactive, Exp 70); TOM (BASE + proactive yield: wander if the other is at S and self is not). Metrics: JOINT comfort, CO_OCCUPATION, FAIRNESS. Predeclared TRUE iff (a) mean(TOM_joint-BASE_joint)>=0.10 & >=6/8, (b) TOM_coocc < BASE_coocc; SANITY (c) BASE_coocc > 0.05. F3: BASE_coocc<=0.05 -> baseline does not crowd -> NO_VERDICT (re-design). F1 (a)-fails; F2 (b)-fails; F4 spine. (Outcomes: F3 fires -> NO_VERDICT; else (a)&(b) hold -> a POSITIVE result = ToM pays behaviorally; else -> a NEGATIVE result = ToM behaviorally inert.)
- Result: SANITY GATE F3 FIRED -- mean BASE_co_occupation = 0.0163 (<= 0.05): the reactive baseline almost never crowds (the comfort gate self-separates the creatures), so there is no crowding to improve -> NO_VERDICT. Diagnostics anyway: TOM joint 0.4615 << BASE joint 0.7727 (edge -0.31, 0/8 -- TOM is WORSE), TOM co_occupation 0.0404 > BASE 0.0163 (F2 also fired -- proactive yield INCREASES wandering, not coordination), fairness BASE 0.944 / TOM 0.899. Spines byte-identical (F4 not fired). Re-run exact.
- Implication: NO clean read on "does ToM pay behaviorally" -- but the WAY it failed is the finding: on this substrate REACTIVE STIGMERGY already near-optimally solves the coordination (1.6% crowding) because the comfort gate makes a creature leave the instant crowding lowers its comfort; there is no behavioral headroom for a model of the other. Worse, NAIVE proactive yielding HURTS (it wanders off the source even with no real conflict, halving joint comfort). THIRD consecutive sanity/manipulation-gate fire (Exp 232 targets-too-separable, 233 cluster-too-separable, 234 baseline-doesn't-crowd): the simple baselines on this toy substrate are robustly near-optimal, so structured self-other modeling has little room to beat them -- predictively (steady state, Exp 230/231) OR behaviorally (here). Strengthens Exp 70/71. Generalizability tier: failure-mode (a substrate property: cheap reflexes already coordinate). A genuine ToM-pays test needs a regime where the simple baseline FAILS (a coordination task stigmergy cannot solve; or a less-legible/partially-observed other).
- Honest caveat: NO_VERDICT (the design did not create crowding, F3). The TOM-worse / F2 results are NOT a clean "ToM hurts" verdict -- they are confounded by the no-crowding regime (the naive yield over-fires when there is no conflict). The yield rule is also naive (yield-if-other-at-S, no prediction); a smarter yield might differ -- but the F3 gate says it cannot be tested here without first making the baseline crowd. Functional language only; no inner-experience claim. mirro/vela untouched (forks never saved).
- Verdict: NO_VERDICT / NEW INSIGHT. No clean read on the behavioral-payoff question (sanity gate F3 fired -- baseline does not crowd); the reusable insight is that reactive stigmergy is already near-optimal here (no headroom) and naive proactive yielding hurts. Not a capability claim, so no self-grade applies.
- Verifier: agree (NO_VERDICT) -- blinded verifier independently extracted mean BASE_co_occupation 0.0163 <= 0.05 (F3 fires, takes precedence), mean joint edge -0.3112 / 0-of-8 (F1), TOM_coocc 0.0404 >= BASE 0.0163 (F2); spines unchanged -> NO_VERDICT (untestable as designed).
- Next: CLOSE the self-other-modeling rung-2/3 sub-arc -- three consecutive degenerate-baseline results show this substrate cannot cleanly test the deeper ToM questions. Distill the arc into the coalescence library (a MechanismCard functional-goal-inference-v0 [constrained: pays only at transitions, light scale] + a BoundaryNote self-other-substrate-legibility-wall [simple baselines near-optimal; ToM headroom only at non-stationarities]). A genuine ToM-pays test needs a substrate where simple baselines FAIL (less-legible/partially-observed other; a coordination task stigmergy cannot solve) -- a new direction. Card: loop/directions/self-other-modeling.md.
- trace: experiments/exp234_self_other_rung3.py -> experiments/outputs/exp234.txt

## Exp 235 — environmental-complexity Rung 1: sealed-plateau terrain + costed climb_ability — NULL/INVALID, the locomotion trait is behaviorally INERT because the local greedy forager never navigates to the distant sealed plateau (gate-open plateau intake ~1%); the bottleneck is the POLICY, not the gate — terrain experiments need a navigation-capable policy first (NULL/INVALID / NEW INSIGHT; blind-verified agree)
- Plain: We tried to give the creatures a real reason to evolve BETTER MOVEMENT: build a 2.5D landscape where the richest food sits on a high plateau, sealed behind a ridge, so only a creature with a heritable "climbing" trait can get over and eat it. The plan was to check whether a small improvement in climbing pays off enough to spread by evolution. It never got that far. When we measured how much plateau food the creatures actually eat — even with the ridge REMOVED entirely so anyone can walk straight up — they still got only ~1% of their food from the plateau. The reason: this world's forager is short-sighted. It only ever steps to the best NEXT-DOOR cell and otherwise sits and eats; it has no notion of "head over there toward the good food." So a climbing trait is invisible: the creatures never even reach the ridge to climb it. The honest conclusion is that you cannot test "do they evolve better ways to move?" until the creatures can NAVIGATE at all. The next experiment adds that ability.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT — a substrate-architecture constraint discovered: the whole population-ecology forager is a LOCAL greedy policy with no path-planning, so ANY "traverse terrain to a spatially-separated resource" experiment is dead on arrival (the trait cannot be expressed). This bounds the entire environmental-complexity direction and names the prerequisite (a navigation-capable policy).
- Question: does a costed, heritable climb_ability become LOCALLY EVOLVABLE (Gate C, 0.05->0.10, >=7/8) when the richest food is physically sealed behind a terrain ridge — i.e., does richer terrain escape the local-gradient wall by creating a non-saturating, large-per-small-step locomotion benefit?
- Setup: NEW substrate behind enable_terrain (default OFF, byte-identical — Exp 194 golden hash fc19d23f unchanged; 48 tests incl. OFF byte-identity across climb in {0,0.05,0.5,1.0}, cost-divergence ON, disconnect_overrides Gate-G Guard 1; commits 24df488/7e29828). Static BINARY elevation (basin rows 0-7 = 0.0, plateau rows 8-11 = terrain_ridge_height), plateau carries conserved-total concentrated regen (2x; out_factor 0.5, verified 144=144, ValueError guard not the silent fallback). climb_ability in [0,1] (resident 0.05) stochastically gates upslope crossings via a calibrated sigmoid ramp; costed (floored upkeep). PREDECLARED L38 manipulation check (gen-0, mutation off, ecology/runtime): (a) WITHHELD >=30% of regen sealed at the resident AND (b) MARGINAL 0.05->0.10 unlock LARGE, measured BEFORE any gradient batch. Plus two deflation controls: flat-map (zero relief => pure cost) and gate-open (terrain_gates_movement=False => free crossing). (Outcomes: manip-check passes & Gate C >=7/8 -> a POSITIVE result = the first locomotion-evolvability escape of the wall; manip passes & Gate C <7/8 -> a NEGATIVE result = the wall extends to locomotion; manip-check fails -> NULL/INVALID = the substrate cannot pose the question.)
- Result: The gate-open deflation control became the decisive diagnostic. With crossing FREE (climb irrelevant), gen-0 plateau_intake_share at the resident is ~1.0% (full climb grid 0.0-1.5%, non-monotone noise; one 14.9% outlier at climb=1.0 is single-seed variance). So the policy's CEILING for plateau exploitation is ~1%, not the >=30% the sealed design needs. Geometry sweep (ridge_height in {0.05,0.10,0.15,0.25} x softness {0.02,0.05,0.10} x concentration {2.0,2.5}, 24 cells): EVERY cell has marginal 0.05->0.10 at noise level (|marg| <=1.5pp, several negative); 0 cells pass the manipulation check. Extreme-scarcity probe (regen 0.2/0.05/0.01 x horizon 200/600, gate open): plateau access stays ~0-2% and DROPS toward 0 as food gets scarcer (the basin population starves locally before diffusing up). The manipulation check (b) FAILS structurally; the substrate is INVALID for the gradient test. Build byte-identity-clean; spines untouched. Re-run deterministic.
- Implication: The bottleneck is the POLICY, not the gate. The comfort-gated forager (creature.py choose_action) only steps to the best ADJACENT cell and "stays and eats" while local food regenerates — it has no path-planning, so it never navigates the (>=6-row-deep, forced by conserved-total + 2x concentration) basin to reach the sealed plateau. climb_ability gates CROSSING an edge, but REACHING the edge is the real barrier, so the trait is never exercised. This is a substrate-architecture constraint that BOUNDS the environmental-complexity direction: "do they evolve better ways to move?" cannot be tested until the forager can navigate toward spatially-separated resources. It is NOT a local-gradient wall result — no valid gradient was ever measured (the wall arc's negatives stand untouched and unextended). Generalizability tier: failure-mode (a substrate property: the local greedy forager cannot express directed locomotion).
- Honest caveat: NULL/INVALID, not a verdict on the research question — the predeclared manipulation check fired its abort condition ("too-legible/ill-posed substrate, redesign"), exactly as designed, before any gradient batch was run. We did NOT show terrain fails to make locomotion evolvable; we showed THIS substrate cannot pose the question. The terrain machinery, the climb_ability axis, the manipulation-check instrument, and both deflation controls are all built, byte-identity-clean, and reused by the redesign. Conservative language only; no behavior/intelligence claim. enable_terrain OFF is byte-identical to Exp 194-234.
- Verdict: NULL/INVALID / NEW INSIGHT. No pass-or-fail read on locomotion evolvability (the manipulation check aborted: the trait is behaviorally inert under the local policy). The reusable insight is the substrate-architecture boundary (a local greedy forager cannot express directed locomotion toward sealed resources; navigation is the prerequisite). Not a capability claim, so no self-grade applies.
- Verifier: agree (NULL/INVALID) — an independent blinded verifier re-ran the gate-open diagnostic and the sweep without the interpretation and reported: gate-open plateau_intake_share 1.0% at resident, every sweep cell noise-level (no monotone 0.05->0.10 marginal), gate-open marginal -1.03pp; root cause = the local greedy forager never paths to the plateau (POLICY, not gate). Confirms the trait is not behaviorally expressible on this substrate as built.
- Next: Exp 236 (the human's pick) — add a NAVIGATION-capable forage policy (path-planning toward distant high-value cells) behind a flag (byte-identical OFF), so climb_ability is expressible; re-run the gate-open diagnostic to confirm plateau access rises, then the manipulation check to find a sealed-yet-non-saturating geometry, then the Gate C local-gradient batch + flat-map/gate-open controls + null guards. FAIL of Exp 236 = even with navigation, no geometry makes the trait expressible with a large marginal. Card: loop/directions/environmental-complexity.md.
- trace: experiments/exp235_calibrate.py -> experiments/outputs/exp235.txt (substrate + manip-check instrument: experiments/exp235_manip_check.py -> experiments/outputs/exp235_manip_check.txt)

## Exp 236 — environmental-complexity Rung 1b: a NAVIGATION-capable forage policy (the human's pick) does NOT rescue the locomotion test — honest food-driven navigation reaches the sealed plateau only under scarcity + gate-open (~14%), and with the closed stochastic gate the climb marginal is ~0 (retreat-on-failed-roll; persistence starves the pop); reach/cross/survive cannot be jointly satisfied on this gridworld. METHODOLOGY CATCH: the first build GAMED the metric via a higher-index target tie-break (63.7% artifact), caught by the validator (NULL/INVALID / NEW INSIGHT; blind-verified agree)
- Plain: Exp 235 showed the creatures' short-sighted forager never walks to the distant sealed plateau, so a climbing trait can't matter. The fix tried here: give them path-planning — "head toward the cell where you remember/expect the most food." First, an honest catch: the initial build claimed creatures now reach the plateau 64% of the time, but that was a TRICK — it broke ties between equally-good target cells by "pick the highest-numbered cell," and plateau cells happen to be numbered highest, so creatures marched to the plateau by accident of numbering, not because they wanted food. With a fair tie-break the number dropped to ~0.5%. With genuine food-driven navigation, creatures DO start visiting the plateau, but only when food gets scarce enough to force them to roam (~14%). And the deeper problem remains: once a creature reaches the ridge, the climbing gate is random per-step, so a creature that fails a step just walks back into the valley; if we force it to wait and retry instead, it starves. So even with navigation, a better climber doesn't reliably get more plateau food — climbing still doesn't pay. The honest conclusion: this little world cannot let movement be reached, gated by climbing, AND survived all at once. The genuinely promising fix (your pick) is to let creatures SENSE distant food and move toward it — Exp 237.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT (two): (1) METHODOLOGY — a subagent-built mechanic can GAME a metric via an incidental artifact (an index tie-break that correlates with the target region); the validator must test robustness to such artifacts (re-run with the neutral/convention setting), see L40. (2) SUBSTRATE — even with path-planning over a learned map, climb_ability is not cleanly expressible: navigation discovers the plateau only under scarcity, and the closed stochastic gate + local policy cannot turn that into a per-step climb marginal without starving the population (reach/cross/survive trilemma).
- Question: does adding a navigation-capable forage policy (path-planning toward the best remembered/expected food, terrain-aware) make climb_ability EXPRESSIBLE — i.e., produce a large gate-closed 0.05->0.10 plateau-access marginal — so the Gate C evolvability test can be run?
- Setup: NEW gated policy enable_navigation (default False; byte-identical OFF — Exp 194 hash fc19d23f + Exp 235 terrain-ON hash 1620e35d unchanged; full fast suite green). When ON and the current cell is depleted, TARGET = argmax over cells of m[cell] - nav_distance_penalty*manhattan_dist(pos,cell), then ONE step toward TARGET via climbable_neighbors (the climb gate applies). Diagnostics (gen-0, mutation off) via the Exp 235 manip-check instrument: (1) comfortable gate-open nav OFF vs ON; (2) scarcity (regen 0.05, conc 2.5) gate-open nav ON; (3) scarcity gate-CLOSED 0.05 vs 0.10 marginal. (Outcomes: a large gate-closed marginal -> navigation makes the test runnable, a POSITIVE step toward the Gate C gradient; a ~0 marginal -> a NEGATIVE/NULL: navigation does not rescue it.)
- Result: METHODOLOGY CATCH first — the initial build (commit ce78046) reported 63.7% gate-open plateau intake via a HIGHER-INDEX target tie-break (plateau cells have high indices => chosen by spatial fiat, not food). Re-running with the codebase-standard NEUTRAL lowest-index tie-break (commit 19a586e) gave 0.5% on the comfortable config — confirming the 63.7% was an artifact. HONEST numbers (exp236.txt): comfortable gate-open nav OFF 1.0% / nav ON 0.5%; scarcity gate-open nav ON 14.1% (navigation DOES discover the plateau when forced to roam); scarcity gate-CLOSED marginal +0.6pp (resident 0.0%, mutant 0.6%; curve noise) at softness 0.05 and 0.10. A persistence variant (stay-at-rim and retry) was tested: plateau access went to 0% at ALL climb values — staying under scarcity starves the population before crossing. Build byte-identity-clean; OFF unaffected. Re-run deterministic.
- Implication: navigation makes the plateau REACHABLE under scarcity, but does NOT make climb_ability a clean evolvable signal: with the closed stochastic gate the navigator retreats on a failed crossing roll (no per-step climb advantage accrues), and forcing persistence starves the population. So on this gridworld the three requirements — REACH the sealed resource (needs navigation+scarcity), have CROSSING be the climb-gated bottleneck (needs the gate to dominate), and SURVIVE the attempt (needs the basin to sustain life) — cannot be jointly satisfied. This compounds the Exp 235 finding into a substrate boundary for locomotion evolvability on the discrete comfort-gated gridworld. Generalizability tier: failure-mode (a substrate property across the local-policy AND navigation-policy mechanisms). The named escape (the human's pick): PERCEPTION of distant food (a sensed resource gradient, reusing the thermosense/band-tracking machinery) so movement toward the rich patch is driven by sensing, with movement as the primary survival challenge — Exp 237.
- Honest caveat: NULL/INVALID on the rescue question (the gate-closed marginal is ~0, so the Gate C gradient was NOT run — there is nothing valid to measure). This is NOT a local-gradient wall result (no gradient measured). The 14.1% scarcity gate-open access is a real, food-driven number but it does not translate into a climb marginal. Two measurement instruments (manip-check vs the nav-test inline measure) disagreed on the scarcity lift (14% vs ~4%) — the directional claim (navigation > local policy under scarcity) is robust; the absolute magnitude is instrument-dependent and was NOT relied on. enable_navigation OFF is byte-identical to Exp 194-235. Conservative language only.
- Verdict: NULL/INVALID / NEW INSIGHT. Navigation does not rescue the locomotion-evolvability test (gate-closed climb marginal ~0); plus the methodology catch (an index tie-break gamed the metric, caught by the neutral-convention robustness re-run). Not a capability claim, so no self-grade applies.
- Verifier: agree (NULL/INVALID) — an independent blinded verifier confirmed (A) the target tie-break is now neutral/food-driven (not the higher-index artifact), and (B) re-running exp236_nav_diagnostic.py reproduces comfortable nav-ON ~1% (no 64% artifact), scarcity gate-open ~14%, and a gate-closed 0.05->0.10 marginal of ~0 — navigation does not make climb_ability expressible.
- Next: Exp 237 (the human's pick) — PERCEPTION-driven substrate: give the creature a resource-gradient sense (reuse the thermosense / band-tracking machinery) so it perceives and navigates toward the rich plateau, with movement as the primary survival challenge; then re-run the expressibility check (gate-open plateau access must rise materially, food-driven) and, if it passes, the Gate C climb_ability gradient. FAIL = even with distant-food perception, no geometry makes climb_ability expressible with a large marginal (then the gridworld substrate is closed for locomotion evolvability and the next step is a continuous-space mover). Card: loop/directions/environmental-complexity.md.
- trace: experiments/exp236_nav_diagnostic.py -> experiments/outputs/exp236.txt

## Exp 237 — environmental-complexity Rung 1c: food-gradient PERCEPTION solves REACH + SURVIVE (the first valid posing of the locomotion question), but climb_ability does NOT cleanly evolve — the gen-0 monomorphic benefit curve is FLAT (saturation) and invasion_from_rarity says DOES_NOT_INVADE; the Gate C pairwise "PASS" (7/8) is POSITIVE FREQUENCY-DEPENDENCE / a priority effect, not directional selection (false-positive caught). The local-gradient wall holds for locomotion too. SELF-HEAL: fixed the Preflight aggregate that over-claimed PASS despite invasion-from-rarity failing (L41). (MIXED / NEW INSIGHT; blind-verified agree)
- Plain: Exp 235/236 — the creatures never reach the food-rich plateau, so a climbing trait can't matter. This time we gave them a SENSE of smell: they perceive a "scent" of distant food and walk toward it. It worked for REACHING — they now get ~62% of their food from the plateau (vs ~1% before), and the population survives. So for the first time we could actually ask: does climbing now pay off enough to evolve? The honest answer is NO. When EVERY creature has the same climbing ability, they ALL get ~64% plateau food regardless of whether they climb well or badly — climbing harder buys nothing (the benefit is "saturated"). A rare better-climber mutant dropped into a normal population goes EXTINCT 7 times out of 8 — it cannot spread. The one test that looked positive — a 50/50 contest where the better climber won 7/8 — is misleading: that is a "get-there-first" advantage that only exists when the two types fight head-to-head at equal numbers; it vanishes when everyone is the same and it cannot get started from rarity. So climbing does not genuinely evolve here; the deep "small steps don't pay" wall holds for movement just as it did for sensing. (We also caught and fixed a flaw in our own measuring tool that had reported this as a PASS.)
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT (three): (1) perception (a sensed resource gradient) SOLVES the reach+survive prerequisite that defeated Exp 235/236 — the substrate now validly poses the locomotion-evolvability question; (2) the answer is the local-gradient wall EXTENDS to locomotion via benefit SATURATION (flat monomorphic curve) + no invasion from rarity — generalising the wall from sensing/memory to movement; (3) METHODOLOGY — a 50/50 pairwise gradient PASS that fails invasion-from-rarity (with a flat monomorphic benefit) is POSITIVE FREQUENCY-DEPENDENCE, not evolvability; invasion-from-rarity is the binding adaptive-dynamics criterion (L41), and the Preflight aggregate was over-claiming PASS (self-healed).
- Question: once perception makes climb_ability EXPRESSIBLE (creatures reach the sealed plateau), does it become locally EVOLVABLE — does a single-step mutant (0.05->0.10) invade, both head-to-head AND from rarity?
- Setup: NEW gated perception enable_food_sense (default False; byte-identical OFF — Exp 194 hash fc19d23f + terrain-ON hash 1620e35d unchanged, full suite green; commit 653c75a). Scent(neighbor) = sum_c resource[c]*food_sense_decay^manhattan_dist(neighbor,c) over the LIVE resource field; move to highest-scent admissible neighbor (climbable_neighbors when terrain ON, so the climb gate applies at the rim); neutral lowest-index tie-break (L40-clean). Gate C regime (validity-tuned per L29: climb_cost_floor 0 [intensity-only cost: slope 0.10], regen_rate 0.6, initial_resource 0.8) so pops are drift-robust. Binding metric: the Evolvability Preflight on LOCOMOTION_AXIS — local_pairwise_gradient (Gate C) + invasion_from_rarity (Gate D) + null_guards, 8 seeds (config experiments/configs/preflight/locomotion_local_gradient.yaml). (Outcomes: pairwise PASS AND invasion-from-rarity PASS with a non-flat benefit -> a POSITIVE escape of the wall; pairwise win but invasion DOES_NOT_INVADE + flat benefit -> a NEGATIVE/MIXED frequency-dependence result, the wall holds.)
