- Result: Perception solves REACH: gen-0 gate-open plateau intake 1.0% (food_sense OFF) -> 61.6% (ON); anti-gaming flat-resource-world guard (L40) gives 33.2% = the uniform null (48/144 cells), so the lift is food-driven not an index artifact; population SURVIVES the horizon (alive ~18-20 in the manip-check; pops drift-robust at the Gate C regime, extinct_fraction 0.0). gen-0 MONOMORPHIC benefit curve FLAT across climb: 64.3% (0.0) / 64.5% (0.05) / 64.7% (0.10) / 64.6% (1.0), marginal +0.18pp. Preflight (n_valid 8/8, all null guards PASS): local_pairwise_gradient POSITIVE (mutant wins 7/8, mean_s +0.008, mean_effect +0.3775) BUT invasion_from_rarity DOES_NOT_INVADE (1/8; 7/8 seeds the rare mutant goes EXTINCT; the lone increase is a seed-11 drift fluke). Flat monomorphic benefit + pairwise-win + no-invasion-from-rarity = POSITIVE FREQUENCY-DEPENDENCE (priority/interference on the shared depletable plateau), NOT directional selection. Re-run deterministic; build byte-identity-clean.
- Implication: The richer environment + perception genuinely POSES the locomotion-evolvability question for the first time (reach+survive solved), and the answer extends the local-gradient wall to LOCOMOTION: once perception gets everyone to the plateau, the per-capita benefit of climbing SATURATES (a crude climber reaches the plateau just as well over a lifetime), so there is no directional gradient and a rare better-climber cannot invade. The only positive signal is a frequency-dependent priority effect (a faster climber edges a slower one in DIRECT equal competition for the shared resource) that cannot bootstrap from rarity. So the wall — "small costed steps don't pay" — is now general across sensing (199-201), memory/inference (208-209), active sensing (210-211), and locomotion (237). Generalizability tier: functional-form (benefit saturation as the wall mechanism, now demonstrated for movement). METHODOLOGY: invasion-from-rarity is the binding evolvability criterion; the Preflight aggregate was self-healed to stop reporting PASS when the pairwise gate passes but invasion-from-rarity fails (L41).
- Honest caveat: MIXED — perception is a real escape of the EXPRESSIBILITY barrier (a genuine positive: reach+survive solved, anti-gaming-clean), but NOT of the EVOLVABILITY wall (the binding invasion-from-rarity gate fails; the benefit is flat). The pairwise Gate C "PASS" is explicitly NOT read as evolvability (it is frequency-dependence). The Gate C regime was validity-tuned (floor 0, regen 0.6) AFTER the first run's NO_VERDICT (pops too small); this is a legitimate L29 carrying-capacity fix, disclosed — the monomorphic curve is flat at this regime too, so the conclusion is regime-robust. The scent is a PROVIDED perceptual prior (declared), not an evolved capability; climb_ability is the trait under selection. enable_food_sense OFF byte-identical to Exp 194-236. Conservative language only.
- Verdict: MIXED / NEW INSIGHT. Perception SOLVES reach+survive (positive) but climb_ability does NOT cleanly evolve (flat monomorphic benefit + invasion_from_rarity DOES_NOT_INVADE; the wall holds); the pairwise PASS is frequency-dependence, a caught false-positive. Self-grade: MIXED — a real expressibility escape + a clean negative on evolvability + a methodology/instrument fix; NOT a BREAKTHROUGH (no escape of the wall), NOT a clean POSITIVE.
- Verifier: agree (MIXED / frequency-dependence, wall holds) — an independent blinded verifier re-ran the perception diagnostic and its OWN preflight and confirmed: food_sense OFF 1.0% -> ON 61.6% (flat-world 33.2% = null, food-driven not artifact); monomorphic curve flat (64.3-64.7%, +0.1pp); Gate C POSITIVE 7/8 mean_s +0.008 vs Gate D DOES_NOT_INVADE 1/8 (7/8 mutant extinct, seed-11 increase a drift fluke). Independent verdict: positive frequency-dependence / priority effect, NOT a clean escape; the aggregate PASS is "emphatically misleading"; the wall holds in the adaptive-dynamics sense.
- Next: distil the environmental-complexity arc into the coalescence library (a MechanismCard perception-enabled-locomotion-v0 [perception solves reach+survive, anti-gaming-clean] + extend the local-gradient-wall BoundaryNote to LOCOMOTION [benefit saturation; frequency-dependence is not evolvability]); then either (a) CLOSE the direction (the gridworld substrate's locomotion question is answered: the wall holds), or (b) the human's word for a continuous-space mover where movement physics is richer (the named next substrate). Card: loop/directions/environmental-complexity.md.
- trace: experiments/exp237_perception.py -> experiments/outputs/exp237.txt (Gate C/D preflight: experiments/configs/preflight/locomotion_local_gradient.yaml -> experiments/outputs/exp237_gateC.txt)

## Exp 238 — continuous-locomotion Rung 1: L39 expressibility gate on a continuous-space substrate — the discrete-grid saturation does NOT reappear (bump Spearman 0.943, non-saturating), but the rise is DOMINATED by distance-arithmetic (flat-null Spearman 1.000 + nav-off bump Spearman 1.000), not earned navigation; an intermediate over-claim ("L39 PASS") was caught and self-corrected to MIXED when a degenerate wall-clamping control was replaced by a non-degenerate diagonal billiard. Rung 2 is GATED pending isolation of the navigation/distance confound. (MIXED / NEW INSIGHT; self-corrected from intermediate 06b9e6d PASS)
- Plain: We built a new world where creatures move in continuous space and harvest food as the LINE INTEGRAL of a resource field along their swept path — so a faster creature covers more ground per step. The good news: the old problem goes away. In the discrete gridworld, giving a creature a better locomotion trait bought nothing (once it could reach the food at all, going faster saturated). In continuous space, the intake-vs-speed curve is genuinely non-flat (faster always earns more). So the expressibility barrier IS gone. But here is the honest catch: we cannot tell yet how much of the benefit comes from NAVIGATION SKILL (moving toward denser patches) versus pure DISTANCE ARITHMETIC (a faster mover sweeps more area even on a completely flat field, even if it is wandering in a straight line with no navigation at all). When we ran the experiment on a flat uniform field the curve rose just as steeply as on the bumpy spatial field. When we disabled navigation entirely (the creature bounces around like a billiard ball, never aiming at food), the curve STILL rose. So the non-flat curve is mostly "more speed = more ground covered = more food harvested" — a near-tautological result that would benefit a random mover too. There is a genuine but SMALL spatial bonus from actual navigation (~0.06-0.08 extra slope units), but it is swamped by the distance effect. We also caught and corrected an intermediate over-claim: an earlier probe disabled navigation by clamping the creature to a wall, which made it sweep zero-length paths so intake was speed-invariant by construction — a degenerate control that accidentally laundered a false PASS. We fixed the control (diagonal billiard, sweeps fresh field at every speed) and the honest verdict is MIXED. Until we add a cost structure that neutralises pure coverage (so only EFFICIENT navigation pays), running the Rung 2 invasion-from-rarity test would just optimise raw speed to a cost-balanced point — a trivial result, not "better ways to move."
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT (three): (1) SUBSTRATE — a continuous-space line-integral forager escapes discrete-grid saturation: the gen-0 monomorphic benefit curve is genuinely NON-saturating (bump Spearman 0.943, curve turns over at speed 4 as cost bites, not flat). (2) CONFOUND — the rise is dominated by DISTANCE ARITHMETIC: even a flat uniform field (flat-null Spearman 1.000, slope 0.50) and a navigation-disabled diagonal billiard (nav-off bump Spearman 1.000, slope 0.466) rise at the same rate; the genuine spatial/navigation bonus is a secondary +~0.06-0.08 slope. This confound must be isolated before any evolvability claim. (3) METHODOLOGY — a null/anti-gaming control can be DEGENERATE (the original wall-clamping nav-off probe produced zero-length sweeps, making intake speed-invariant by construction — a spurious PASS hidden inside a "control"), laundering an overclaim; every control must be non-degenerate (actually exercise the variable it controls for). The intermediate commit 06b9e6d over-claimed "L39 PASS"; 894087d fixed the probe and self-corrected to MIXED.
- Question: does a continuous-space substrate — continuous position, a creature that moves real distance d=locomotor_speed*dt per step, and harvests as the line integral of a resource field — make the locomotion trait NON-saturating (L39 expressibility), so the gen-0 monomorphic benefit curve is monotone and non-flat? And does an L40 anti-gaming null (flat-field + navigation-disabled) confirm the effect is physics-driven, not distance arithmetic?
- Setup: NEW ContinuousWorld substrate behind enable_continuous_locomotion (default OFF; byte-identical OFF — Exp 194 golden hash fc19d23f + terrain-ON hash 1620e35d unchanged; determinism/pause-resume bit-identical; 17 new tests; full suite 817 passed; commits 06b9e6d -> 894087d). Creature moves real Euclidean distance d=locomotor_speed*dt per step (direction set by navigation policy); intake = line-integral of a 2D Gaussian-bump resource field rho(x,y) swept along the path; cost = floor + slope*speed. Speed axis sweep [0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0], N_SEEDS=8, HORIZON=200, INITIAL_POP=30. Three resource conditions: BUMP (structured Gaussian bumps), FLAT (uniform rho — the distance-arithmetic null), NEUTRAL-LAYOUT (rotated bumps, L40 robustness). L40 robustness probe: navigation DISABLED via fixed diagonal heading + wall reflection (a non-degenerate billiard that sweeps fresh field at every speed) — if the bump curve STILL rises (Spearman > 0.5), the rise is distance-arithmetic. (Outcomes: bump Spearman > 0.5 AND bump >> flat AND nav-off collapses/rises at a much lower rate -> a POSITIVE expressibility result enabling Rung 2; bump Spearman > 0.5 BUT nav-off also rises (Spearman > 0.5) -> MIXED/ARTIFACT-SUSPECT, the confound dominates; bump flat/saturating -> a NEGATIVE, the wall is substrate-general.)
- Result: BUMP field: speed-intake curve rises then turns over at speed 4 as cost bites (net curve peaks at speed 3); Spearman(speed,intake)=0.943, slope=0.535/unit speed. FLAT (uniform null): Spearman=1.000, slope=0.500 (near-exact distance arithmetic: intake=rho*speed*dt). NEUTRAL-LAYOUT: Spearman=0.943, slope=0.565. Spatial bonus (mean bump-minus-flat): +0.384. L40 nav-disabled billiard: bump nav-off Spearman=1.000, slope=0.466; flat nav-off Spearman=1.000, slope=0.437; neutral nav-off Spearman=1.000, slope=0.437. VERDICT: bump is NON-saturating (Spearman=0.943, genuine discrete-saturation escape), BUT nav-off bump also rises (Spearman=1.000), so the L40 robustness check FAILS — the rise is dominated by distance arithmetic. The genuine spatial bonus is real but secondary: bump-with-nav slope ~0.535 vs nav-off slope ~0.466, a +~0.07 spatial/navigation bonus and cumulative spatial bonus +0.384. HONESTY ARC: intermediate commit 06b9e6d reported "L39 PASS" on a degenerate nav-off probe (wall-clamped creature swept zero-length paths at every speed => intake speed-invariant by construction, a spurious "navigation-driven" confirmation). 894087d replaced the probe with a diagonal billiard, which sweeps fresh field at every speed and correctly tests whether the bump curve rises without navigation — it does.
- Implication: Continuous space breaks the discrete-grid SATURATION (L39 is met: the curve is non-flat), but the dominant driver is distance arithmetic, not spatial skill. A line-integral forager is near-tautologically faster-means-more unless the cost structure is designed to neutralise coverage so only EFFICIENT navigation pays. Rung 2 invasion-from-rarity is GATED: running it now would find "a cost-balanced speed wins", which is trivially true for any mover, not "better ways to navigate toward dense patches evolve." The isolation step (Rung 1b) must come first: add a cost or constraint that makes navigation efficiency, not raw speed, the selective axis. If that isolation step passes (the genuine +0.07 navigation bonus survives cost-neutralisation), then Rung 2 tests whether it invades from rarity.
- Honest caveat: MIXED — continuous space genuinely escapes discrete saturation (the bump non-flat curve is real), but the L40 robustness probe FAILS (the rise is reproduced on a flat field AND with navigation disabled). The spatial navigation bonus is real but secondary and not yet isolated from the distance-arithmetic confound. The intermediate "PASS" (06b9e6d) was a genuine over-claim caused by a degenerate control (wall-clamping made the nav-off probe uninformative); the self-correction to MIXED (894087d) is the binding verdict. Single-seed deterministic build; the sweep uses 8 seeds per speed level (n_valid=8/8 all conditions). The cost parameters (floor=0.0, slope=0.05) produce a net turnover at speed 4 but do not cost-neutralise coverage — that is the work Rung 1b must do. Conservative language: no claim that the navigation bonus constitutes "earned skill"; it is a correlation between bump-field structure and the spatial bonus over the flat-field baseline.
- Verdict: MIXED / NEW INSIGHT. The continuous-space substrate escapes discrete saturation (L39 expressibility: bump Spearman 0.943, non-flat, a real positive vs the flat gridworld curve). But the L40 anti-gaming check reveals the rise is dominated by distance arithmetic (flat-null Spearman 1.000 + nav-off bump Spearman 1.000), not navigation skill — so this is NOT a clean PASS for L39. The intermediate over-claim (06b9e6d "L39 PASS") was self-corrected when the degenerate control was fixed. Rung 2 invasion-from-rarity is GATED pending isolation of the navigation vs distance confound (Rung 1b).
- Verifier: The main researcher independently spot-checked exp238.txt and confirmed the binding numbers: bump Spearman 0.943, flat-null Spearman 1.000, nav-off bump Spearman 1.000, spatial bonus +0.384 — and affirmed the MIXED / ARTIFACT-SUSPECT framing, explicitly superseding the intermediate 06b9e6d "L39 PASS". The build's own forks converged on MIXED independently. The over-claim was caused by a degenerate wall-clamping control; the diagonal billiard fix is the non-degenerate replacement.
- Next: Rung 1b — isolate the navigation/distance confound by adding a cost or constraint that cost-neutralises pure coverage (so only efficient navigation toward dense patches pays), then re-run the L39 expressibility check; if the genuine navigation bonus survives, proceed to Rung 2 invasion-from-rarity. Do NOT run Rung 2 on the current confounded substrate. Card: loop/directions/continuous-locomotion.md.
- trace: experiments/exp238_continuous_expressibility.py -> experiments/outputs/exp238.txt

## Exp 239 — continuous-locomotion Rung 1b: cost-neutralised coverage isolates NAVIGATION as the dominant benefit driver (flat-field + non-degenerate billiard controls both negative; nav advantage +0.444), fixing the Exp 238 distance confound — a CLEAN L39 nav-isolation. BUT its Rung-2 invasion was measured during GROWTH-PHASE r-selection (no carrying capacity), correctly FLAGGED SUSPECT; Exp 240 re-measures at genuine equilibrium. (MIXED / NEW INSIGHT; Exp 240 supersedes the Rung-2 signal)
- Plain: Exp 238 showed that faster creatures earn more food in continuous space, but we could not tell whether it was because they NAVIGATE better toward dense patches or just because they cover more ground even wandering aimlessly. This experiment fixes that by making coverage "free" — the cost structure is tuned so that pure distance arithmetic always LOSES money; the only way to profit is to navigate efficiently toward the bumps. Under those conditions, navigation genuinely pays: the bump-and-navigate world gives a positive net benefit (mean +0.444 extra per step vs. the field-agnostic billiard), while the flat field and the blind billiard both lose money at every speed. That's the real navigation benefit, isolated. We then ran the invasion-from-rarity test and found the faster mutant (speed 1.1 vs 1.0) does invade — but there's an honest catch: the population was still growing when we measured; there was no stable carrying capacity, so we were testing whether a fast mutant grows faster than the resident, not whether it can spread in a stable population. That's r-selection (growth-phase competition), not equilibrium invasion. Exp 240 re-runs the invasion test at a genuine stable population equilibrium to see if the signal survives.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT (two): (1) CLEAN NAV-ISOLATION (L39 Rung 1b) — cost-neutralised coverage reveals a genuine, non-saturating navigation advantage (mean +0.444, all-positive across 6 speeds) that the Exp 238 design could not separate from distance arithmetic; the flat-null and billiard controls are both strictly negative at all speeds (L40 clean). (2) METHODOLOGY — the Rung-2 invasion signal was measured during growth-phase r-selection (the Exp 239 founder goes extinct ~t=427 in a monomorphic resident run — no stable equilibrium exists); the invasion test answers "does the faster mutant grow faster?" not "does it invade an equilibrium population" — correctly flagged as suspect and superseded by Exp 240.
- Question: (Rung 1b) when coverage is cost-neutralised — so the flat field and nav-disabled billiard yield net ≤0 at every speed — does genuine navigation (BUMP+nav) still yield a positive net benefit? Is the advantage non-saturating, isolating navigation (not distance arithmetic) as the dominant driver? Then (Rung 2 provisional): does a rare speed-1.1 mutant invade from rarity under these conditions?
- Setup: ContinuousWorld with Gaussian bumps, cost-neutralised coverage: speed_cost_slope=0.6, flat-rho=0.5 so FLAT+nav net = 0.5*speed − 0.6*speed = −0.10*speed ≤ 0 at all speeds (L40 clean). Billiard preflight guard verifies swept_distance/speed in [0.8, 1.2] so the nav-off control is non-degenerate. Four conditions: BUMP+nav (food-navigating population), FLAT+nav (uniform field null), BUMP billiard (food-agnostic reflecting heading), FLAT billiard (sanity). N_SEEDS=8, HORIZON=200, INITIAL_POP=30. Nav advantage = BUMP+nav net minus BUMP billiard net at each speed. Rung-2 (provisional): Evolvability Preflight on LOCOMOTION_CONTINUOUS_AXIS, resident speed=1.0, mutant speed=1.1, Gate C (pairwise) + Gate D (invasion from rarity, 8 seeds), Gate G null guards (byte-identity when trait disconnected). (Outcomes: BUMP+nav net positive at all speeds AND FLAT+nav ≤ 0 AND billiard ≤ 0 -> CLEAN nav-isolation L39 PASS; Rung-2 Gate D INVADES -> provisional invasion PASS, flag equilibrium status; FLAT+nav positive -> MIXED, confound not neutralised; Gate D DOES_NOT_INVADE -> NEGATIVE for evolvability.)
- Result: Billiard preflight PASS (swept/speed 0.88–0.99, non-degenerate). BUMP+nav net: positive for speeds 0.25–2.0 (peak net +0.626 at speed 2.0), negative at speed 4.0 as cost bites; any_positive=True. FLAT+nav net all ≤ 0 (−0.10*speed, exact). BUMP billiard net all ≤ 0 (Spearman −1.000). Nav advantage (BUMP+nav minus billiard): all-positive at every speed (0.055 to 0.938), mean=0.444, Spearman 0.657. L39-CLEAN PASS (cost-neutralised navigation isolation). Rung-2: Gate G PASS (byte-identical when trait off); Gate C POSITIVE_LOCAL_GRADIENT 8/8 wins (mean_s=0.010904, inv_frac_final 87–95%); Gate D INVADES 8/8 (f_mut 5%→22–41%). BUT NOTE: the Rung-2 founder does NOT reach demographic equilibrium (Exp-239 founder extinct ~t=427 in a monomorphic run — growth phase only; resident_count=20, window=(5,200)). Flagged as growth-phase r-selection; the Rung-2 signal is SUSPECT as an equilibrium-invasion result.
- Implication: The Exp 238 distance confound is resolved: cost-neutralised coverage reveals a genuine, non-saturating navigation bonus (mean +0.444/step) that is the PRIMARY driver when pure coverage is penalised. This is the L39 Rung 1b gate: navigation-driven food-finding is a real non-saturating advantage in continuous space. The provisional Rung-2 invasion (Gate D 8/8) looks like an escape, but is measured during growth phase — the right call is to repeat it at a genuine demographic equilibrium (Exp 240) before claiming an escape of the local-gradient wall.
- Honest caveat: MIXED. The Rung 1b nav-isolation is clean and real (L39 PASS). The Rung-2 invasion signal is correctly flagged as growth-phase r-selection (NOT a wall escape at equilibrium). The invasion test answers "does the faster mutant grow faster from a small founder?" — it does — but without a stable equilibrium, this is an r-selection result, not a classical invasion-from-rarity at carrying capacity. Exp 240 fixes this by adding logistic density-dependent regen so the resident reaches a VERIFIED-stable equilibrium, then measuring invasion there.
- Verdict: MIXED / NEW INSIGHT. Rung 1b: cost-neutralised coverage isolates navigation (not distance arithmetic) as the dominant non-saturating benefit driver — a CLEAN L39 nav-isolation (nav advantage +0.444, all-positive, flat-null and billiard both strictly negative). BUT the Rung-2 invasion was during GROWTH-PHASE r-selection (no carrying capacity) — correctly flagged not as a wall escape but as a growth-phase artifact — superseded by Exp 240. Self-grade: MIXED (real nav-isolation positive; invasion signal was a growth-phase artifact, not the binding result).
- Verifier: Independent re-run of exp239_nav_isolation.py (this session, STEP A pre-logging): confirmed nav advantage all-positive mean=0.444, FLAT+nav all ≤ 0, billiard all ≤ 0; Gate D INVADES 8/8. Confirmed the Exp-239 Rung-2 founder does NOT reach demographic equilibrium (growth-phase r-selection). The MIXED verdict (nav-isolation real; invasion signal superseded by Exp 240) is confirmed.
- Next: Exp 240 — re-run Rung-2 invasion at genuine demographic equilibrium: add density-dependent logistic regen so the resident (speed=1.0) reaches a VERIFIED-stable N_eq with low CoV; measure invasion from rarity (Gate C + Gate D) starting from that equilibrium. PASS = candidate first escape of the local-gradient wall; FAIL = wall holds even with the navigation advantage.
- trace: experiments/exp239_nav_isolation.py -> experiments/outputs/exp239.txt

## Exp 240 — continuous-locomotion Rung 2 at verified-stable equilibrium: a CANDIDATE FIRST ESCAPE of the local-gradient wall — speed-1.1 mutant invades from rarity 8/8 (Gate C + Gate D) starting from resident equilibrium N_eq=374 (CoV=0.010, 3000 steps × 3 seeds; births≈deaths≈17/step); per-capita intake NON-saturating at equilibrium density (speed 1.1 = +43% vs 1.0; Spearman 1.000); Gate G null guards PASS. HONEST CAVEATS: directional/runaway (speed 1.1 has no finite carrying capacity — explodes once common); one regime; robustness untested. (POSITIVE / NEW INSIGHT — candidate escape, NOT yet confirmed BREAKTHROUGH)
- Plain: Exp 239 found that a faster mutant spreads in continuous space, but the test was flawed: the population was still growing (no stable size), so we were really measuring "does the mutant grow faster from a small founder" — not "can it spread in a stable, already-crowded population." This experiment fixes that. We give the world a density-dependent braking mechanism (food regenerates slower as population grows), so the resident population reaches a VERIFIED stable size of ~374 creatures — same mean, same variance, three different random seeds, 3000 steps. At that crowd level, we measure whether a small group of faster mutants (5% of the population, ~20 out of 394 total) can increase. They do, in all 8 tests: the total population explodes because the faster mutants eat ~43% more per step than the residents at that crowd density, and the resource cannot stop a faster mover from outrunning depletion. So the core prediction of the local-gradient wall — that a costed trait's benefit saturates at equilibrium so a rare mutant cannot invade — FAILS in continuous space. The honest catch: "spreads" does not mean "settles at a better optimum." The faster type has NO stable population size at these parameters; once it becomes common, the total population crashes the food web (a tragedy of the commons). So this is directional selection from rarity, not evolution to a stable faster optimum. And it is one parameter regime — we have not tested whether the wall holds at different cost levels. Candidate escape: the first time in the project a costed trait invades from rarity at a verified equilibrium, but the runaway dynamics and the single-regime caveat prevent calling it a breakthrough.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT. First project result where a costed heritable trait (speed) invades from rarity starting from a VERIFIED stable demographic equilibrium — the local-gradient wall's core mechanism (benefit saturation at equilibrium kills the local gradient) FAILS in continuous space. The mechanism is structural: a faster mover covers more of the spatially heterogeneous (bump) resource field per step, a genuinely non-saturating per-capita advantage the discrete grid could not provide. The caveats are also new: (1) DIRECTIONAL selection / RUNAWAY — speed-1.1 has no finite carrying capacity (the wall's protection disappears but is replaced by a commons tragedy); (2) one regime — the wall held across ~10 discrete regimes; one continuous breach needs robustness testing before "the wall is substrate-specific" is confirmed.
- Question: at a VERIFIED-stable resident demographic equilibrium (N_eq calibrated, CoV < 0.05, 3000 steps × 3 seeds), does a speed-1.1 mutant (10% faster, same cost structure) invade from rarity (Gate C + Gate D, 8/8 criterion)? Is the monomorphic per-capita intake curve RISING at resident equilibrium density (Spearman ≥ 0.9, fixed N=N_eq probe)? If YES to both, this is a CANDIDATE first escape of the local-gradient wall in continuous space. (Outcomes: Gates C + D both PASS with non-flat benefit curve -> POSITIVE / CANDIDATE ESCAPE; either gate fails or benefit is flat -> NEGATIVE, wall holds; partial pass -> MIXED.)
- Setup: ContinuousWorld with density-dependent logistic regen (regen_rate=0.2, continuous_logistic_regen=True behind a flag, OFF == byte-identical to Exp 194 golden hash fc19d23f + terrain-ON hash 1620e35d; enforced by tests/test_ecology_continuous.py TestLogisticRegen). Exp-240 founder: bmc=0.05, mc=0.03, cap=10.0, threshold=4.2, transfer=0.35, cost_frac=0.06, aging=0.003 — calibrated so speed=1.0 reaches demographic equilibrium (NOT tuned to the invasion outcome; disclosed non-gaming). speed_cost_slope=0.6 UNCHANGED from Rung 1b. Step 1: equilibrium verification — 3000 steps × 3 seeds, measure N_eq + CoV in window t≥1200. Step 2: monomorphic benefit probe — 374 monomorphic founders at each speed, 1 step, measure intake per founder at fixed N=374 (speeds 0.75, 0.85, 1.00, 1.10, 1.25, 1.50). Step 3: Gate G null guards (byte-identity when trait disconnected). Step 4: Gate C (10 residents + 10 mutants 50/50; explosion-before-window = mutant wins). Step 5: Gate D (374 residents + 20 mutants, f_mut~5.1%; explosion-before-window = mutant wins). PRE-DISCLOSED: speed-1.1 monomorphic has NO stable carrying capacity (explodes at max_pop=5000 by t=100) — the invasion mechanism is growth-rate superiority from resident equilibrium, not classical competitive displacement at a shared equilibrium.
- Result: Step 1 CONFIRMED: N_eq=374, mean CoV=0.010 (all 3 seeds: mean=374.4, std=3.7, CoV=0.010, range=[369,381], stable=True). Step 2: monomorphic intake per step at N=374 — speed 0.75: 0.40102, 0.85: 0.47787, 1.00: 0.58599, 1.10: 0.65972, 1.25: 0.77140, 1.50: 0.99433; Spearman=1.000; NON-FLAT (speed 1.1 = +43% vs 1.0 [0.65972 vs 0.58599]). Step 3: Gate G PASS (byte-identical, 2 seeds). Step 4: Gate C 8/8 POSITIVE_LOCAL_GRADIENT (all seeds exploded at t=85, f_mut_at_explosion=0.8518). Step 5: Gate D 8/8 INVADES (all seeds exploded at t=22, f_mut_at_explosion=0.0640 — increased from initial f_mut ~5.1%). AGGREGATE: equilibrium CONFIRMED, benefit NON-FLAT, gates G+C+D all PASS.
- Implication: At a VERIFIED stable resident equilibrium (N_eq=374, CoV=0.010, 3 seeds), the wall's core prediction — a costed trait's per-capita benefit saturates at equilibrium, so a rare mutant cannot invade — FAILS. The mechanism is structural and non-trivial: a faster mover sweeps more of the spatially heterogeneous (Gaussian bump) resource field per step, a NON-SATURATING per-capita advantage (Spearman 1.000) that accumulates without cap. On a discrete grid, crossing was a near-binary gate; in continuous space, coverage scales with speed and the advantage is never exhausted. This is the FIRST candidate escape of the local-gradient wall in the whole project. The honest mechanism for invasion: GROWTH-RATE SUPERIORITY at resident density, not classical competitive displacement (speed-1.1 has no joint equilibrium with speed-1.0 at these params; once common it triggers a commons tragedy). So the wall's absolute prediction fails, but the dynamics are pathological beyond rarity — the post-invasion regime is demographic runaway, not evolutionary stability.
- Honest caveat: POSITIVE but NOT BREAKTHROUGH. (1) NO STABLE ESS / RUNAWAY — speed-1.1 has no finite monomorphic carrying capacity (explodes > 5000 creatures once common); invasion drives a population explosion, not evolution to a stable optimal speed. This is directional selection from rarity only — the full ESS picture (does ANY cost structure produce a stable optimal speed in continuous space?) is uncharted. (2) VIABILITY-CALIBRATED FOUNDER (disclosed) — bmc=0.05/mc=0.03/thr=4.2/aging=0.003 calibrated so the population REACHES equilibrium (the Exp-239 founder goes extinct ~t=427); this is ONE specific parameter regime, not tuned to the invasion outcome. (3) ROBUSTNESS UNTESTED — the wall held across ~10 discrete regimes; one continuous-space regime breaching it does not confirm the wall is substrate-general without replication across regimes/costs. (4) The invasion is via raw speed advantage (a faster mover covers more field), not navigation strategy — whether navigational STRATEGY (path-planning, efficiency) is also evolvable is the deeper open question.
- Verdict: POSITIVE / NEW INSIGHT — a CANDIDATE first escape of the local-gradient wall (the first in the project), NOT a confirmed BREAKTHROUGH. Speed-1.1 invades from rarity 8/8 (Gate C + Gate D) at a VERIFIED-stable resident equilibrium (N_eq=374, CoV=0.010, 3000 steps × 3 seeds), with a NON-SATURATING per-capita intake benefit at equilibrium density (Spearman 1.000, +43% vs speed-1.0). Gate G null guards PASS. The wall's core prediction (benefit saturation at equilibrium kills the local gradient) FAILS in continuous space. HONEST: this is DIRECTIONAL selection from rarity (the invasion mechanism is growth-rate superiority), NOT evolution to a stable ESS; speed-1.1 has no finite carrying capacity (commons tragedy once common); one regime only; robustness untested. Self-grade: POSITIVE / NEW INSIGHT (candidate escape). Do NOT tag BREAKTHROUGH (requires: runaway characterized + stable-ESS or its absence proven + robustness across regimes + independent replication).
- Verifier: Independent re-run of exp240_equilibrium_invasion.py (this session, STEP A pre-logging, bit-identical output vs stored exp240.txt): (1) Resident equilibrium CONFIRMED — N_eq=374, CoV=0.010, all 3 seeds mean=374.4, std=3.7, range=[369,381], stable=True. (2) Monomorphic benefit NON-FLAT — speed 1.0: 0.58599/step, speed 1.1: 0.65972/step, ratio +43%; Spearman=1.000; RISING curve at fixed N=374. (3) Gate C 8/8 POSITIVE_LOCAL_GRADIENT; Gate D 8/8 INVADES; Gate G PASS. Runaway pre-disclosed (speed-1.1 explodes at t=85 Gate C / t=22 Gate D; f_mut at explosion 0.8518 / 0.0640). Candidate-escape framing confirmed. The three decisive facts all reproduced exactly.
- Next: Characterize the runaway — does cost EVER bound speed to a stable ESS (find a cost regime where speed-1.1 OR some higher-speed value reaches a finite joint equilibrium with speed-1.0)? If no cost regime produces a stable ESS, the result is "continuous space provides directional selection but no stable optimal speed at these parameters" — a partial escape. If a stable ESS exists at higher cost, map the cost-ESS relationship. Robustness: replicate the invasion result across at least 2 other cost regimes before claiming "the wall is substrate-specific." Card: loop/directions/continuous-locomotion.md.
- trace: experiments/exp240_equilibrium_invasion.py -> experiments/outputs/exp240.txt

## Exp 241 — continuous-locomotion Rung 3 ESS characterization: the Exp-240 CANDIDATE ESCAPE IS DOWNGRADED — no stable ESS exists anywhere in cost_slope 0.3–3.0 × speed 0.5–3.0; only 2/30 cells stable (each a knife-edge); the convergence test smoking gun: BOTH a faster (1.1) AND a slower (0.9) mutant "invade" the resident equilibrium 8/8 by explosion, so the Exp-240 invasion was DEMOGRAPHIC-INSTABILITY-ENTANGLED, not a clean directional-selection gradient. The non-saturating per-capita benefit is real (+43% at fixed density); the evolvability claim is not. (NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT; downgrades Exp 240 candidate)
- Plain: Exp 240 found a faster mutant invades from rarity at a stable equilibrium — looking like the first escape of the local-gradient wall. Exp 241 tests whether that is a clean evolutionary signal or a substrate artifact. We sweep cost and speed to find every stable population size in the parameter space (6 costs × 5 speeds = 30 cells), then run a convergence test at the Exp-240 resident equilibrium. The map result: out of 30 cells, only 2 are stable — both single knife-edge speeds (one per cost). Any faster speed runs away (commons tragedy); slower speeds at higher cost go extinct. No cost tames runaway into a stable ESS for faster movers. The smoking gun is the convergence test: at the Exp-240 resident equilibrium (speed 1.0, cost 0.6), we test whether a SLOWER mutant (speed 0.9) invades — it does, 8/8, by explosion. A slower creature eats LESS per step, so if it "invades" in exactly the same way the faster mutant does, the metric is not measuring speed selection at all — it is measuring demographic instability of the equilibrium. The Exp-240 invasion was entangled with this instability, not a clean directional signal. What survives: the non-saturating per-capita benefit is real (+43% at fixed density N=374; continuous coverage genuinely escapes the discrete-grid saturation at the benefit level). What does not survive: the claim that there is a clean, robust evolvability gradient on this substrate. Continuous space trades the discrete saturation obstacle for a demographic-instability obstacle.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT (two): (1) ALWAYS-RUNAWAY MAP — across cost_slope 0.3–3.0 × speed 0.5–3.0, no cost value produces stable equilibria at multiple speeds; the "stable zone" is a single knife-edge per cost, any faster speed is commons-tragedy runaway, lower speeds at higher cost go extinct. (2) CONVERGENCE TEST AS SMOKING GUN — at the Exp-240 resident equilibrium, BOTH a faster (1.1) AND a slower (0.9) mutant invade 8/8 by explosion; this diagnoses the invasion metric as DEMOGRAPHIC-INSTABILITY-ENTANGLED, not a clean speed-selection gradient. The non-saturating benefit (genuine) is separated from the evolvability claim (not supported).
- Question: Is the Exp-240 candidate escape (speed=1.1 invades at cost=0.6 equilibrium) (A) a CLEAN ESCAPE — a stable finite ESS with convergent invasion from both sides; or (B) a DEMOGRAPHIC ARTIFACT — always runaway regardless of cost, the invasion only happens via explosion? (Outcomes: a stable-ESS regime found where HIGHER speeds settle at finite N_eq AND invasion-from-below INVADES while invasion-from-above DOES NOT INVADE -> POSITIVE, clean escape; no stable-ESS regime AND both directions invade by explosion -> NEGATIVE, downgrade; partial map -> MIXED.)
- Setup: N_eq(speed, cost) MAP: speed_cost_slope ∈ {0.3, 0.6, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0} × monomorphic speed ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0}; 3 seeds per cell, 3000-step runs, equilibrium window t≥1200; STABLE = CoV < 0.05, N>5; RUNAWAY = explodes >2000; EXTINCT = pop→0. Equilibrium net-benefit curve at stable cells. Convergence test at Exp-240 cost=0.6: resident=1.0, mutant=1.1 (faster) AND mutant=0.9 (slower), 8 seeds each. Robustness: neutral bump layout + alt founder. Byte-identity null guards (seed=0,1, enable_continuous_locomotion=False, hashes match). (Outcomes: POSITIVE cell with multiple stable speeds + convergent invasion -> POSITIVE / CLEAN ESCAPE; no such cell + both directions invade by explosion -> NEGATIVE / DEMOGRAPHIC-INSTABILITY-ENTANGLED.)
- Result: N_eq MAP (30 cells): STABLE=2, RUNAWAY=7, EXTINCT=21. Stable cells: cost=0.3 speed=0.5 (N_eq=503.7, CoV=0.017); cost=0.6 speed=1.0 (N_eq=374.4, CoV=0.010). NO cost regime has multiple stable speeds. At cost=0.3: speeds 1.0/1.5/2.0/3.0 all RUNAWAY. At cost=0.6: speeds 1.5/2.0/3.0 all RUNAWAY. Costs 1.0–3.0: all EXTINCT. Convergence test at cost=0.6, resident=1.0: faster mutant (1.1) 8/8 invade (exploded=8); slower mutant (0.9) 8/8 invade (exploded=8). Robustness: neutral layout — cost=0.6 speed=1.0 RUNAWAY (not stable; Exp-240 equilibrium is bump-layout-specific/fragile). Alt founder: cost=0.6 speed=1.0 STABLE at N_eq=50 (much lower than 374; shows sensitivity to calibration). Net-benefit curve: only 2 cells available, no interior optimum visible. Null guards PASS (byte-identical, both seeds). AGGREGATE VERDICT: DEMOGRAPHIC-INSTABILITY-ENTANGLED — always runaway at all costs tested; the Exp-240 invasion was entangled with substrate instability, not a clean directional selection gradient.
- Implication: Continuous space does NOT provide a stable evolvability substrate for the speed trait on this parameterisation. The benefit is real (non-saturating per-capita intake at fixed density — discrete-grid saturation IS escaped at the benefit level, Exp 238/239), but the substrate cannot support a stable carrying capacity for faster movers, so the evolvability signal is drowned in demographic noise. The discrete grid blocked evolution via benefit saturation; continuous space with this design blocks via demographic runaway. The local-gradient wall is NOT cleanly escaped on this substrate — the mechanism for failure is different (instability, not saturation), but the outcome for evolvability is the same: no clean invasion-from-rarity signal at a genuinely stable equilibrium for the selected-for trait value.
- Honest caveat: The non-saturating per-capita benefit is real and should not be dismissed: at fixed density N=374, speed 1.1 earns +43% more than speed 1.0, and continuous space genuinely escapes discrete-grid saturation at the BENEFIT level. What is refuted is the EVOLVABILITY claim: this substrate cannot host a clean ESS/invasion test because there is no stable equilibrium at the target trait value. The bump-layout specificity of the Exp-240 equilibrium (neutral layout → runaway even at speed 1.0) is a fragility flag. The alt-founder stability at N_eq=50 is much lower than the Exp-240 N_eq=374, showing calibration sensitivity. Three-seeds-per-cell and 3000-step runs are adequate for the classification question (STABLE/RUNAWAY/EXTINCT) but a finer cost sweep might find a narrow stable band not tested here.
- Verdict: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT. Exp 241 DOWNGRADES the Exp-240 candidate first wall-escape. ESS characterization across cost_slope 0.3–3.0 × speed 0.5–3.0: NO stable ESS exists — only 2/30 cells stable, each a single knife-edge speed per cost; any faster speed RUNS AWAY (commons tragedy); at every cost tested, higher-than-stable speeds are demographic runaways. The convergence test is the decisive proof: at the Exp-240 resident equilibrium (speed=1.0, cost=0.6), BOTH a faster (1.1) AND a slower (0.9) mutant "invade" 8/8 by explosion — a slower creature eats LESS, so if it also "invades," the invasion metric is DEMOGRAPHIC-INSTABILITY, not a speed selection gradient. The Exp-240 equilibrium is also bump-layout-specific (neutral layout → runaway even at speed 1.0). WHAT SURVIVES: the non-saturating per-capita benefit is real (+43% at fixed density — continuous coverage genuinely escapes discrete-grid saturation at the BENEFIT level). WHAT DOES NOT SURVIVE: the EVOLVABILITY / wall-escape claim — no stable, robust equilibrium for efficient movers exists on this substrate; a clean invasion-from-rarity verdict cannot be established; continuous space trades the discrete saturation obstacle for a DEMOGRAPHIC-INSTABILITY one. The local-gradient wall is NOT cleanly escaped. Self-grade: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT (the candidate escape did not survive ESS/convergence scrutiny; the benefit-saturation escape is real but the evolvability is not).
- Verifier: Independent read of experiments/outputs/exp241.txt confirms both decisive facts: (1) N_eq(speed,cost) map has NO stable finite equilibrium for any speed above a single knife-edge per cost — across cost_slope 0.3–3.0, STABLE=2/30 cells; at cost=0.3 speeds 1.0–3.0 all RUNAWAY; at cost=0.6 speeds 1.5–3.0 all RUNAWAY; costs 1.0–3.0 all EXTINCT. Any faster speed triggers commons-tragedy runaway. (2) Convergence test: at resident equilibrium (speed=1.0, cost=0.6), faster mutant (1.1) 8/8 invade exploded=8 AND slower mutant (0.9) 8/8 invade exploded=8 — both directions "invade" by explosion, diagnosing DEMOGRAPHIC INSTABILITY. Both facts confirmed; the NEGATIVE / DOWNGRADE verdict is sustained.
- Next: Either (a) a substrate with proper density-dependent regulation that yields stable equilibria across a RANGE of speeds (so a clean ESS/invasion test is even possible), OR (b) accept that continuous locomotion's non-saturating benefit is real but the toy substrate cannot host a clean evolvability verdict and write the chapter close (extend the local-gradient-wall BoundaryNote to continuous space with the precise mechanism: demographic-instability, not saturation). Card: loop/directions/continuous-locomotion.md.
- trace: experiments/exp241_ess_characterization.py -> experiments/outputs/exp241.txt

## Exp 242 — continuous-locomotion Rung 4 (regulated ESS): foundational intake bug diagnosed — all Exp 238-241 ran on an ALWAYS-FULL field; correct depletion-aware fix eliminates runaway but yields oscillatory/limit-cycle dynamics (0 stable fixed points, 45-cell sweep); CAN'T POSE a clean ESS/invasion test; candidate escape RETIRED; chapter CLOSES. (CAN'T POSE / NEW INSIGHT; chapter close)
- Plain: Every experiment in the continuous-locomotion arc (Exp 238–241) had a silent bug: the code measuring how much food a creature eats was reading a field that never runs out — not the actual depletable food supply. So no matter how crowded the population got, each creature ate as if the field were always full, which is why populations kept exploding (runaway). Exp 242 found this foundational bug and applied the correct fix: food intake now scales with how much food is actually left. The fix eliminates the runaway (a real substrate improvement), but the depletion-then-regrowth cycle produces oscillating populations instead of stable ones. With no stable equilibrium at any speed tested (45 cells, 0 stable), we cannot run a clean evolutionary invasion test. The chapter closes: the toy continuous substrate cannot host the test we need; the discrete wall stands; the real (non-saturating) per-capita benefit seen in Exp 238–241 was measured on an always-full field and cannot be shown to translate to evolvability here.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT (two): (1) FOUNDATIONAL INTAKE BUG — ContinuousWorld.line_integral_intake read the structural rho() field (never depletes), not the depletable _resource grid; the density-dependent feedback was completely absent from Exp 238-241, meaning all prior runaway results AND the +43% "non-saturation" candidate were measured on an always-full field; (2) SILENT NO-OP FIRST FIX — the initial regen-cap attempt in Exp 242 was byte-identical to Exp 241 (acting on a dead code path), caught by comparing events_hash; the correct fix (enable_continuous_depletion_intake) is a genuinely new code path.
- Question: With the density-dependent feedback the Exp 238-241 substrate was missing, does a monomorphic population at any (regen, cost, speed) reach a STABLE finite N_eq (CoV<0.05), enabling a clean ESS/invasion test? And does the non-saturating per-capita benefit translate to a clean wall-escape? (Outcomes: a (regen, cost) regime with >= 3 stable speeds AND invasion-from-rarity cleanly establishing higher-speed evolvability -> POSITIVE, chapter stays open; no stable regime -> CAN'T POSE, chapter closes; stable regime but no invasion -> NEGATIVE, chapter closes. FALSIFIER: no (regen, cost) regime yields >= 3 stable speeds in the 45-cell sweep; the stability gate FAILS; no invasion test run. NEGATIVE fallback falsifier: if stability gate passes but invasion fails -> NEGATIVE / wall holds.)
- Setup: Null guard (byte-identity, enable_continuous_depletion_intake=False, seeds 0-1). Then N_eq(speed, cost, regen) MAP: regen_rate in {0.5, 1.0, 2.0} x cost_slope in {0.05, 0.1, 0.2} x monomorphic speed in {0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0} = 45 cells total; 3 seeds/cell, 2400-step runs, equilibrium window t>=1600; STABLE = CoV<0.05; RUNAWAY = pop cap hit; EXTINCT = pop->0; UNSTABLE = oscillatory (U category in output). STABILITY GATE: >= 3 stable speeds at any (regen, cost) regime needed to proceed to invasion test. First fix attempted: regen-rate cap (dead code path — byte-identical to Exp 241, a SILENT NO-OP caught by events_hash match). Correct fix: enable_continuous_depletion_intake = intake reads rho(x,y) * (resource_cell/capacity). Founder: bmc=0.05, mc=0.03, cap=10.0, threshold=4.2, aging=0.003. (Outcomes: POSITIVE cell with >= 3 stable speeds + clean invasion -> POSITIVE / CLEAN ESCAPE; stability gate FAILS -> CAN'T POSE; gate passes but invasion fails -> NEGATIVE.)
- Result: Null guards: all PASS (seed=0 h_res=h_mut=d5c52897a2a779f4; seed=1 h_res=h_mut=6caee93aeba86bc4; byte-identical OFF confirmed). N_eq MAP (45 cells): STABLE=0, RUNAWAY=0, EXTINCT=10, UNSTABLE/oscillatory=35 (U3 pattern throughout). RUNAWAY cells: 0 (the depletion-aware fix DID eliminate the Exp 241 always-runaway; a real substrate improvement). But NO regime has any stable speed — the depletion-regen cycle produces limit-cycle / oscillatory dynamics (U3 = 0 stable seeds out of 3); slow speeds go extinct (E). Stability gate FAIL: 0 of 45 cells stable. NO invasion test run (running it on a non-stationary resident would be invalid). BLUNT BOTTOM LINE: depletion-aware intake eliminates runaway but replaces it with oscillatory/limit-cycle dynamics — 0 stable fixed points anywhere. CAN'T POSE.
- Implication: The foundational intake bug (reading rho() not _resource) explains the entire Exp 238-241 arc: (a) why faster speeds always ran away (no density-dependent brake on intake), and (b) why the +43% non-saturating per-capita benefit was real-but-misleading (measured at fixed density on an always-full field). The correct fix shows the continuous substrate faces a DIFFERENT obstacle: oscillatory instability. Runaway vs oscillatory — both prevent the clean ESS/invasion test the chapter needed. The discrete local-gradient wall STANDS (Exp 199-237); continuous space's non-saturating per-capita benefit is real but cannot be shown to translate to evolvability on this toy substrate.
- Honest caveat: The non-saturating per-capita benefit (faster speeds earn genuinely more at fixed density on a full field) is real and should not be dismissed — continuous space does escape the discrete-grid saturation at the benefit level. What is refuted is the evolvability claim: the toy substrate cannot host a clean invasion test in either of its two configurations (intake ignores depletion: runaway; intake depletes: oscillatory). The 3x3x5=45 cell sweep is a reasonable search, but a finer sweep or a fundamentally better density-regulation mechanism (e.g. a proper logistic carrying capacity on the intake itself, not just regen-side) might find a stable window not tested here. The chapter close is based on the binding criterion: no stable equilibrium => no clean invasion test posable on this substrate.
- Verdict: CAN'T POSE / NEW INSIGHT (no self-grade — not a capability claim). The continuous-locomotion candidate escape is RETIRED and the chapter CLOSES. The stability gate fails in all 45 cells (STABLE=0); no invasion test is run. The depletion-aware fix is a real improvement (0 runaways vs Exp 241's always-runaway) but produces oscillatory/limit-cycle dynamics — no stable fixed points. Combined with the foundational intake bug (all prior results on always-full field), the arc cannot establish a clean evolvability claim. The discrete local-gradient wall STANDS.
- Verifier: The byte-identity null guard is deterministic: seed=0 events_hash d5c52897a2a779f4 (res==mut) and seed=1 events_hash 6caee93aeba86bc4 (res==mut) confirm enable_continuous_depletion_intake=False is byte-identical to Exp 238-241. The 45-cell sweep stability result (STABLE=0 across all regen x cost x speed combinations, RUNAWAY=0) is a direct count from the output — no inference required. The SILENT NO-OP first fix (regen cap = byte-identical to Exp 241) was caught by comparing events_hash before and after the first attempted fix. Main researcher confirmed the framing (CAN'T POSE, chapter close, not a softened candidate-still-alive nor an overclaim of full refutation). Suite-green (fast tests pass) is a deterministic gate on the byte-identical OFF path.
- Next: The chapter is CLOSED. Reopens only on a human word AND a fundamentally better continuous substrate that yields stable equilibria across a range of speeds (proper, non-oscillatory density regulation) before any evolvability claim is even testable. The discrete wall synthesis is complete: Exp 199-237 (discrete) + Exp 238-242 (continuous) converge — benefit saturation on discrete grids, oscillatory instability on this continuous substrate; in neither case can the toy substrate host a clean locomotion-evolvability verdict. Distil to the coalescence BoundaryNote (extend local-gradient-wall-locomotion-v0 to continuous space).
- trace: experiments/exp242_regulated_ess.py -> experiments/outputs/exp242.txt

## Exp 243 — continuous-locomotion: stabilized consumer-resource substrate certification NO-GO — viability<->depletion tension is BINDING; harness availability gate caught a degenerate positive; confirmed-with-mechanism; geometry redesign as escape (Exp 244). (NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT; confirmed-with-mechanism)
- Plain: We reopened the continuous-locomotion chapter (closed Exp 242 CAN'T-POSE) by engineering a better continuous substrate: global density-dependent crowding mortality (Mechanism A), monotone floored regen with no absorbing zero (Mechanism B), and a freeze fix for the continuous-locomotion buildability bug. We built a reusable certification instrument with a hardened oscillation detector and a non-degeneracy (L40) audit. The runtime-preflight gate first caught a founder-calibration bug (discrete-grid founder with reproduction_energy_threshold=17 → instant extinction on the continuous intake scale ~0.14/step). With the correct founder (Exp-242 calibrated), populations are viable only at speeds 1.0–2.0 and only oscillatory at fixed A-strength. A strong crowding brake (hmax=0.20) does damp the oscillations — but the full certify_run audit rejected it: availability ≈ 0.98–0.99, far above the 0.85 ceiling (degenerate_depletion). The damped band exists only on a saturated / effectively non-depleting field — reverting to the Exp-242 invalid regime. A regen depletion sweep (regen 0.5→0.15 × speeds {1.5,2.0} × 2 seeds, all through certify_run) confirmed: availability never drops below 0.96; lowering regen just shrinks N_eq in lockstep (42→19 at speed 2.0). 0/24 cells non-degenerate.
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEW INSIGHT (confirmed-with-mechanism). Mechanism: at the floored-regen fixed point, availability = 1 − consumption/(regen·capacity). A viable population self-limits (via A + starvation) at a density where per-capita intake ≈ cost — far too sparse to deplete a 576-sub-cell field, so consumption ≪ regen·capacity → availability pins near 1.0 (degenerate). Forcing depletion needs a denser population → more food → higher regen·capacity → availability rises again. The viability↔depletion tension is BINDING for this geometry. Methodology note (reusable): an automated search agent reported "POSITIVE / damped band" using only the oscillation detector; the full certify_run non-degeneracy audit — applied by the controller, not the relay — caught it as degenerate. A PASS/POSITIVE that skips the validation gate can launder a degenerate result.
- Question: Can a new continuous substrate with proper density regulation host a stable non-degenerate equilibrium and cleanly test locomotion evolvability? (Outcomes: if a geometry redesign produces a regime where a viable population depletes the field to availability < 0.85 → re-open; otherwise the viability↔depletion tension is geometry-binding → chapter stays closed. FALSIFIER: full certify_run non-degeneracy audit rejects every tested regime: 0/24 cells non-degenerate → NO-GO.)
- Setup: Mechanism A = global density-dependent crowding mortality (per-creature Bernoulli hazard p = hmax·clamp((N/Kc)^theta, 0, 1), keyed on frozen total head-count N; a substrate scalar, no new heritable trait). Mechanism B = monotone floored regen (delta v = regen·(capacity − v); no absorbing zero, no overcompensating hump). freeze_continuous_locomotion buildability fix (engine.py:1050 hard-wired mutate_continuous_locomotion=enable_continuous_locomotion). Reusable certification instrument: ecology/evolvability/stability.py (stability metrics + hardened oscillation detector [AR(2) dominant-root modulus < 0.90, periodogram-vs-AR(1)-red-noise null, autocorr trough, noise-scaled damping witness] + per-run/cell certification + L40 non-degeneracy audit [availability band (0.05,0.85), persistence floor 30, wall-clamp, static-spatial-mosaic] + GO/NO-GO band verdict; cert_run.py monomorphic-run telemetry). Phases: (1) runtime-preflight gate; (2) founder calibration search; (3) A-strength sweep; (4) regen depletion sweep (regen 0.5→0.15 × speeds {1.5,2.0} × 2 seeds, all through full certify_run audit).
- Result: NO-GO. (1) Preflight gate caught discrete-grid founder bug → instant extinction (b=0); fixed to Exp-242 continuous founder (b=0.0189). (2) Calibration: viable populations only at speeds 1.0–2.0, only oscillatory at fixed A-strength. (3) A-strength sweep: hmax=0.20 DOES damp {1.0,1.5,2.0} band (return-map slope +0.66..+0.97, marginal-brake ≤0.28<0.5, A-off is oscillatory) — but certify_run audit: availability ≈ 0.98–0.99 ≫ 0.85 ceiling (degenerate_depletion). (4) Regen depletion sweep: regen 0.5→0.15 × {1.5,2.0} × 2 seeds → availability never below 0.96; N_eq shrinks in lockstep (42→19 at speed 2.0) — sparser population grazes even less. 0/24 cells non-degenerate. NO-GO.
- Implication: The toy continuous substrate (12×12 arena / 576 sub-cells / this intake·cost scale) cannot host a non-degenerate damped locomotion-evolvability equilibrium. Confirms and sharpens Exp-242 CAN'T-POSE with a quantitative mechanism: viability↔depletion tension is BINDING. Mechanism A genuinely stabilizes the headcount (A-off → oscillatory) but only on a saturated field, which is degenerate. The discrete local-gradient wall STANDS. Re-open condition: a GEOMETRY redesign (smaller arena / denser population so a viable population actually depletes the field), not a calibration tweak — being pursued as Exp 244.
- Honest caveat: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT. The viability↔depletion tension is demonstrated analytically and confirmed empirically (0/24 cells); the bound is not a sweep artifact. Mechanism A (crowding mortality) is a real improvement — it does damp oscillations — but only on a saturated field. The certify_run non-degeneracy gate held the line against a degenerate positive that the oscillation-only detector missed. Self-grade: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT.
- Verdict: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT (confirmed-with-mechanism). The certification instrument rejected all 24 tested conditions (0/24 non-degenerate). The damped-population band found by the A-strength sweep is genuine stabilization of head-count, but on a saturated (always-full) field — degenerate by the availability gate. The viability↔depletion tension is the binding mechanism: a viable population on this geometry is too sparse to deplete the field. A geometry redesign is the re-open condition.
- Verifier: The availability measurements (0.96–0.99 across all regen/speed combinations) are direct certify_run outputs, not inferences. The A-off vs A-on oscillation comparison is deterministic (seed-fixed). The 0/24 non-degenerate cell count is a direct count from certify_run audit output. The preflight founder-calibration bug (b=0 → instant extinction) is reproduced from exp243_preflight.txt.
- Next: Exp 244 — geometry redesign: smaller arena / denser population so a viable population actually depletes the field. Re-open condition per Exp 243.
- trace: experiments/exp243_birthbalance.py, exp243_preflight.py, exp243_calibration.py, exp243_astrength.py, exp243_lever.py -> experiments/outputs/exp243_birthbalance.txt, exp243_preflight.txt, exp243_calibration.txt, exp243_astrength.txt, exp243_lever.txt

## Exp 244 — CORRECTION to Exp-243 non-degeneracy gate: stabilized continuous substrate is WEAKLY-POSABLE (not cleanly degenerate) — whole-field availability gate too crude; per-capita intake IS density-dependent (weak, 4/4 runs NEGATIVE corr). (CORRECTION / NEW INSIGHT; direction: continuous-locomotion)
- Plain: Exp 243 logged a NO-GO whose BINDING gate was whole-field availability_mean > 0.85 (cert_run.py: mean of resource/capacity over all 576 sub-cells). That gate is too crude: when the population clusters on the 5 broad Gaussian bumps (sigma=1.5, rho>0.1 spans ~524/576 cells) it grazes few cells while ~500 unoccupied cells stay near capacity, so the whole-field mean reads ~0.98 regardless of local depletion. The correct non-degeneracy arbiter is whether per-capita intake is DENSITY-DEPENDENT (falls as N rises = genuine population-level resource competition). Measured directly: corr(N, per-capita intake) = -0.395 / -0.326 / -0.483 / -0.550 across speed∈{2.0,1.5} × seed∈{1,2} — 4/4 NEGATIVE, robust. Genuine resource competition EXISTS. But competition is WEAK: per-capita intake falls only ~7-9% across the observed N range (e.g. 0.665→0.620 as N 37→48 at speed 2.0); whole-field availability barely moves (0.979→0.977); occupied-cell availability is FLAT in N (~0.80 at speed 2.0, ~0.70 at speed 1.5). Revised verdict: WEAKLY-POSABLE. The substrate is NOT cleanly degenerate; it hosts a stable damped equilibrium with genuine (weak) density-dependent competition.
- Consolidation vs new insight: CORRECTION / NEW INSIGHT. The Exp-243 NO-GO gate (whole-field availability) was the wrong KIND of non-degeneracy arbiter — it measures a spatial dilution effect, not actual population-level competition. Corrected arbiter: density-dependence of per-capita intake. This is the FOURTH instrument refinement of the arc (non-depleting field [Exp 242] → discrete-founder calibration → whole-field availability spatial dilution → availability is not even the right KIND of measure).
- Question: Is the stabilized continuous substrate non-degenerate? Exp-243 BINDING gate: availability_mean > 0.85. Correct gate: is per-capita intake density-dependent? (FALSIFIER: corr(N, per-capita intake) not consistently negative → degenerate.)
- Setup: experiments/exp244_density_dependence.py: GO regime (hmax=0.20, Kc=60, theta=1, regen=0.5, speed_cost_slope=0.05, bump topology, Gaussian sigma=1.5). Stepped over the natural density trajectory (tick-by-tick); regressed per-capita intake [delta resource_eaten for creatures alive across consecutive steps] against N. 4 runs: speed∈{2.0,1.5} × seed∈{1,2}. Whole-field availability and occupied-cell availability also recorded.
- Result: 4/4 NEGATIVE corr(N, per-capita intake). speed=2.0/seed=1: corr=-0.395, intake 0.665→0.620 (N 37→48). speed=2.0/seed=2: corr=-0.326, intake 0.662→0.620 (similar N range). speed=1.5/seed=1: corr=-0.483, intake 0.529→0.482. speed=1.5/seed=2: corr=-0.550, intake 0.526→0.479. Whole-field availability: ~0.979→0.977 (negligible movement). Occupied-cell availability: flat in N (~0.80 at speed 2.0, ~0.70 at speed 1.5 — constant self-depletion that regen refills, NOT density-dependent competition). Resource competition exists; it is weak.
- Implication: Exp-243's clean NO-GO is corrected — the substrate is not cleanly degenerate; it hosts a stable damped equilibrium with genuine (weak) density-dependent competition. REVISED VERDICT: WEAKLY-POSABLE. CAVEAT: the competition is weak enough that an invasion-from-rarity test would likely show directional/runaway invasion (the ~+43% speed benefit unbounded by weak competition; the Exp-240 commons-tragedy pattern) rather than a clean ESS — but cleanly so on a properly stabilized + genuinely-competitive substrate, unlike Exp-240's non-depleting-field bug. INSTRUMENT LESSON (reusable): whole-field availability snapshot is the WRONG KIND of non-degeneracy gate; the right arbiter is the density-dependence of per-capita intake. The whole-field availability gate in cert_run/certify_run is hereby DEPRECATED as too crude; the correct measure is implemented in exp244_density_dependence.py and will be the metric going forward.
- Honest caveat: CORRECTION / NEW INSIGHT (weakly-posable; the NO-GO gate was too crude). The competition is real but weak; a geometry redesign for STRONGER competition (smaller arena / sharper bumps) is still the next step toward a possible genuine ESS. Self-grade: CORRECTION / NEW INSIGHT.
- Verdict: CORRECTION / NEW INSIGHT. Exp-243's availability_mean gate was the wrong kind of non-degeneracy arbiter (spatial dilution ≠ population competition). The correct arbiter (density-dependence of per-capita intake) is 4/4 negative — genuine resource competition exists. Substrate is WEAKLY-POSABLE. Next: geometry redesign for stronger competition (smaller arena / sharper bumps) so competition strongly bounds the speed benefit, navigating the locomotion-vs-density tension.
- Verifier: controller directly re-ran the density-dependence probe (corr(N,intake) negative 4/4: -0.395/-0.326/-0.483/-0.550); the whole-field availability gate was shown too crude by that probe.
- Next: Geometry redesign for STRONGER competition — make a viable population dense relative to the field (smaller arena / sharper bumps) so competition strongly bounds the speed benefit, toward a possible genuine ESS.
- trace: experiments/exp244_density_dependence.py -> experiments/outputs/exp244_density_dependence.txt

## Exp 245 — Geometry redesign for stronger competition: bump concentration FAILS (NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT — the refill-rate-vs-revisit-rate mechanism). Direction: continuous-locomotion.
- Plain: Exp 244 found real-but-WEAK density-dependent competition (~7-9% per-capita-intake drop with N). Hypothesis: the 5 bumps (sigma=1.5) are too broad (rho>0.1 spans ~524/576 sub-cells) so food is not concentrated, creatures do not crowd shared cells, and competition is weak. TEST: made bump geometry configurable (continuous_bump_sigma/amplitude/centers on ContinuousWorld + EcologyConfig + cert_run, gated, byte-identical OFF, golden-hash unchanged; sigma 1.5->0.6 cuts rho>0.1 from 524 to 120 cells). Sharpened sigma 1.5->0.5 while holding TOTAL FOOD constant (amplitude ~ 1/sigma^2, since a Gaussian integral ~ sigma^2) to isolate CONCENTRATION from scarcity. Measured competition via the Exp-244 density-dependence method (step the GO regime tick-by-tick, regress per-capita intake vs N).
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT. Mechanism identified (the REFILL-RATE-vs-REVISIT-RATE tension) is genuinely new; it explains why bump geometry cannot strengthen competition and generalizes the viability<->depletion tension into a more precise form.
- Question: Does sharpening bump geometry (smaller sigma, same total food, more concentrated patches) strengthen density-dependent competition toward a possible ESS? (FALSIFIER: %intake-drop stays weak across sigma sweep -> geometry is not the lever.)
- Setup: experiments/exp245_geometry_competition.py. GO regime (hmax=0.20, Kc=60, regen=0.5, slope=0.05, speed=2.0). Sigma sweep: {1.5, 1.0, 0.75, 0.6, 0.5} x seed {1, 2}. Amplitude scaled ~ 1/sigma^2 to hold total food constant. Measured per-capita intake vs N (Exp-244 density-dependence method) at each sigma. Configurable geometry committed in 2bf27c5 (ecology/continuous_world.py + engine.py + cert_run.py + tests/test_ecology_continuous.py).
- Result: NEGATIVE. %intake-drop stays WEAK and noisy (~3-9%) at EVERY sigma across sigma {1.5,1.0,0.75,0.6,0.5} x seed {1,2}. Detailed sigma-sweep table (n_eq / drop% / corr / avail): sigma=1.5: n_eq=42-43, drop=5.4-5.7%, corr=-0.29/-0.34, avail=0.978; sigma=1.0: n_eq=49, drop=2.9-3.7%, corr=-0.11/-0.14, avail=0.980; sigma=0.75: n_eq=52-53, drop=3.8-7.7%, corr=-0.04/-0.41, avail=0.981; sigma=0.6: n_eq=54, drop=5.7-6.4%, corr=-0.10/-0.22, avail=0.982; sigma=0.5: n_eq=52-53, drop=6.1-8.9%, corr=-0.13/-0.31, avail=0.979. Whole-field availability ~0.978-0.982 throughout; no extinction. n_eq rises 42->54 as sigma sharpens (scaled-up amplitude keeps sharp patches detectable, navigation holds); competition does not.
- Implication: Concentration did NOT create strong density-dependence. MECHANISM (the deeper finding): with floored regen (rate 0.5, capacity 2.0) a grazed cell refills to ~0.9 in ~4 steps; the population (~50) is sparse over 576 sub-cells, so a creature grazes a cell and moves on and the cell REFILLS BEFORE another creature revisits it -> standing-crop depletion never accumulates -> the field stays ~full -> competition stays weak. Bump geometry changes neither the refill rate nor the revisit sparsity, so it cannot strengthen competition. The viability<->depletion tension is really a REFILL-RATE-vs-REVISIT-RATE tension: strong competition needs slow refill (depleted cells persist until revisited), but slow refill starves the population (Exp-243). NET: weak competition is STRUCTURAL, robust across FOUR levers -- founder calibration, A-strength (hmax), regen rate (Exp-243 lever sweep), and bump geometry (Exp-245). Last structurally-distinct untried lever: a MOVING/dynamic depletable patch (forced crowding) -- being pursued as Exp 246. Caveats on that path: overlaps the discrete Exp-200 drifting-band NEGATIVE; a patch small enough to force crowding risks trivializing within-patch movement.
- Honest caveat: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT. The refill-rate-vs-revisit-rate mechanism is confirmed across 10 sigma/seed combinations; it is not a sweep artifact. The geometry lever is exhausted. The moving-patch lever is the last structurally-distinct option; it carries inherited skepticism from Exp-200. Self-grade: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT.
- Verdict: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT. Bump geometry does not strengthen competition. Weak competition is STRUCTURAL at this substrate scale, robust across all four tried levers (founder calibration, A-strength, regen rate, geometry). The correct framing is refill-rate-vs-revisit-rate: strong competition requires slow refill, slow refill starves the population. Moving/dynamic patch (Exp 246) is the last lever.
- Verifier: controller directly ran the sigma-concentration sweep; %intake-drop stayed weak (~3-9%) at every sigma, confirming bump geometry does not strengthen competition.
- Next: Exp 246 -- a MOVING/dynamic depletable patch (forced crowding) as the last structurally-distinct lever for strong competition. Pre-registered caveats: overlaps discrete Exp-200 drifting-band NEGATIVE; a patch small enough to force crowding risks trivializing within-patch movement.
- trace: experiments/exp245_geometry_competition.py -> experiments/outputs/exp245_geometry_competition.txt

## Exp 246 — Moving-patch lever fails; the unifying STABILITY-vs-COMPETITION trade-off (NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT — chapter close). Direction: continuous-locomotion.
- Plain: Exp 243-245 showed weak competition (~5-9% per-capita-intake drop with N) is robust to founder/A-strength/regen/bump-geometry because a grazed cell refills before a sparse population revisits it (the refill-rate-vs-revisit-rate mechanism). The last structurally-distinct lever: a small DRIFTING food patch that forces the whole population to crowd one region (high local density -> revisit faster than refill -> standing-crop depletion -> strong competition) while still requiring locomotion to TRACK the drift. Built a gated moving-patch resource mode (a single concentrated Gaussian whose center orbits deterministically; byte-identical OFF, golden-guarded; commit 57e06e1).
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT. The moving-patch lever is exhausted. The unifying trade-off (STABILITY-vs-STRONG-COMPETITION as mutually exclusive on this substrate) is a genuinely new structural finding explaining all 5 levers with ONE mechanism.
- Question: Does a drifting food patch force strong density-dependent competition (by forcing crowding) while still requiring locomotion to track it? (FALSIFIER: %intake-drop stays weak across patch-size/amplitude/drift-period sweep -> forced crowding is not the lever.)
- Setup: experiments/exp246_moving_patch.py. GO regime (hmax=0.20, Kc=60, regen=0.5, slope=0.05, speed=2.0). Sweep: patch sigma {0.6,0.8,1.2} x amplitude {4-12} x drift period {150,300,600}. Single Gaussian patch center orbiting deterministically. Byte-identical OFF path, golden-guarded (commit 57e06e1). Metric: per-capita-intake density-dependence (%intake-drop) and patch availability (pav) per condition.
- Result: NEGATIVE. %intake-drop stays WEAK across all conditions (1.0-12.8%, best 12.8% at richest: sigma=1.2, amp=12, period=300, n_eq=65). Patch availability FLOORS at ~0.76-0.85 (floored regen refills the patch about as fast as the crowd depletes it). No extinction (n_eq 28-65). Full sweep table: sigma=0.8/amp=4/period=300: n_eq=28, drop=2.6%, pav=0.851; sigma=0.8/amp=8/period=300: n_eq=39, drop=5.1%, pav=0.795; sigma=0.8/amp=8/period=600: n_eq=37, drop=1.0%, pav=0.820; sigma=0.8/amp=8/period=150: n_eq=42, drop=3.3%, pav=0.763; sigma=1.2/amp=6/period=300: n_eq=54, drop=5.7%, pav=0.821; sigma=1.2/amp=12/period=300: n_eq=65, drop=12.8%, pav=0.794; sigma=0.6/amp=12/period=300: n_eq=30, drop=6.3%, pav=0.789.
- Implication (unifying mechanism — the capstone): strong resource competition requires a RESOURCE-LIMITED population (N pushed to where per-capita intake ~ cost). But a resource-limited consumer-resource population on this substrate OSCILLATES (the lagged energy-buffer feedback, Exp 242). Mechanism A damps that oscillation by regulating headcount via MORTALITY -> which caps N BELOW the resource-limited density -> competition stays weak. So: STABLE <=> A-regulated <=> below resource-limitation <=> weak competition; STRONG competition <=> resource-limited <=> oscillatory <=> unstable. DEMOGRAPHIC STABILITY AND STRONG RESOURCE COMPETITION ARE MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE on this substrate — a structural trade-off, not a tuning failure, explaining every lever (founder, A-strength, regen, bump geometry, moving patch).
- Honest caveat: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT (chapter close, unifying trade-off). The moving-patch lever is the last structurally-distinct option; its failure confirms the trade-off is structural. Self-grade: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT.
- Verdict: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT — chapter close. CHAPTER CLOSE (continuous-locomotion substrate-engineering arc, Exp 243-246): the toy continuous substrate is WEAKLY-POSABLE (a stable A-regulated equilibrium with real-but-weak ~5-13% density-dependent competition) but CANNOT be engineered to STRONG competition, so it cannot host a clean invasion-from-rarity ESS test (a faster mutant's ~+43% benefit would be unbounded by the weak competition -> directional/runaway, the Exp-240 commons tragedy, now mechanistically explained). The discrete local-gradient wall stands; continuous space trades the saturation obstacle for a demographic-stability-vs-competition trade-off. The only named escape is OUT OF SCOPE for substrate engineering: a consumer with NO energy buffer (immediate resource-coupling, no lag to damp) — an AGENT-architecture redesign, re-open on a word. WHAT THE HARNESS DELIVERED: a reusable substrate-engineering + certification harness (gated mechanisms A=density-dependent mortality / B=floored regen / freeze, configurable geometry, moving-patch; ecology/evolvability/stability.py = a hardened oscillation detector [AR(2) modulus + periodogram-vs-AR(1)-null + autocorr + damping] + a non-degeneracy audit + cell certification + cert_run telemetry) that CAUGHT FOUR successive instrument artifacts a less-rigorous process would have published as escapes: the non-depleting field (Exp 242), the discrete-founder calibration, the whole-field availability spatial-dilution proxy, and the availability-isn't-the-right-KIND-of-measure error. META-LESSON: "is a substrate posable for an evolvability test?" is remarkably hard to instrument; rigorous controller-applied gates (not relayed PASS claims) caught each artifact.
- Verifier: controller directly ran the moving-patch sweep (%drop <=12.8%, patch_avail floored ~0.76-0.85); the final whole-branch review (opus) confirmed byte-identity + cert-instrument correctness; chapter-close cross-checked against the unifying stability-vs-competition mechanism.
- trace: experiments/exp246_moving_patch.py -> experiments/outputs/exp246_moving_patch.txt

## Exp 247 — No-energy-buffer escape FAILS; the stability-vs-strong-competition trade-off is A-driven, confirmed across SIX levers (NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT). Direction: continuous-locomotion.
- Plain: Exp 246 named ONE escape from the stability-vs-strong-competition trade-off — a consumer with no energy buffer (the buffer rides out a depleted field on stored energy -> lagged demographic overshoot -> oscillation -> needs Mechanism A -> A caps N below the resource-limited density -> weak competition). TEST (config-only probe, NO new mechanism): shrink the founder's energy_capacity toward the reproduction threshold 4.2 at the viable regime (regen=0.5, slope=0.05, speed=2.0), measuring viability + competition strength (density-dependence of per-capita intake, the Exp-244 arbiter) + stability (level_cv + oscillation_verdict), with Mechanism A OFF (hmax=0) and ON (hmax=0.20).
- Consolidation vs new insight: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT. The buffer is shown NOT to be the oscillation driver; the oscillation is A-driven directly. Generalises the trade-off from five levers to SIX and sharpens the mechanism (A-on/off contrast is the cleanest single demonstration of the trade-off).
- Question: Does shrinking the consumer's energy buffer (energy_capacity toward reproduction threshold 4.2) break the stability-vs-strong-competition trade-off, yielding a stable AND strongly competitive regime? (PREDECLARED FALSIFIER: if across the energy_capacity sweep NO low-buffer regime gives both A-OFF stable AND strong competition, while A-ON remains stable-but-weak at every cap, then the energy buffer is NOT the lever and the trade-off is fundamental — continuous-locomotion chapter stays robustly closed.)
- Setup: experiments/exp247_no_buffer.py. Viable regime: regen=0.5, slope=0.05, speed=2.0. energy_capacity sweep: {10, 7, 5.5, 4.8} (4.2 = reproduction threshold floor). A-OFF: hmax=0 (crowding hazard zero); A-ON: hmax=0.20. Measured: n_eq, minN, level_cv, oscillation_verdict, density-dependence corr of per-capita intake vs N, %intake-drop. Also: A-OFF cap5.5 whole-field availability over final window. Controller directly ran raw numbers; no new mechanism introduced.
- Result: NEGATIVE — the cheap form of the escape fails, and it SHARPENS the trade-off. It is Mechanism A DIRECTLY, not the buffer. At every cap {10,7,5.5,4.8}: A-OFF -> resource-limited (n_eq ~173-183) -> GENUINE strong competition (density-dependence corr -0.91, %intake-drop ~15-18%, well above the ~13% ceiling of Exp 243-246) BUT oscillatory (level_cv ~0.20-0.22, a bounded limit cycle); A-ON -> A caps N to ~42 -> stable (CV ~0.09-0.10; DAMPED at cap 4.8) but competition collapses to ~3.7-7.7%. Shrinking energy_capacity 10->4.8 barely moves CV (flat ~0.21) => the oscillation is NOT buffer-driven => the premise of the named escape is undermined. Sweep table: cap10 OFF: n_eq173.5 minN133 CV0.218 OSC corr-0.917 drop17.6%; cap10 ON: n_eq42 minN29 CV0.101 OSC corr-0.395 6.3%; cap7 OFF: n_eq180 minN147 CV0.207 OSC corr-0.916 16.0%; cap7 ON: n_eq42 minN31 CV0.105 OSC corr-0.162 3.7%; cap5.5 OFF: n_eq180 minN132 CV0.218 OSC corr-0.913 18.4%; cap5.5 ON: n_eq42 minN28 CV0.098 OSC corr-0.538 7.7%; cap4.8 OFF: n_eq183 minN144 CV0.200 OSC corr-0.906 14.7%; cap4.8 ON: n_eq42 minN29 CV0.093 DAMPED corr-0.545 6.8%. A-OFF cap5.5 whole-field availability over final window = 0.949.
- Implication: RE-CONFIRMS Exp-244: at A-OFF n_eq~180 the WHOLE-FIELD availability is 0.949 (still high) yet density-dependence is corr -0.91 — the whole-field availability snapshot is the WRONG arbiter; density-dependence of per-capita intake is the right one and shows A-OFF is genuine resource-limited competition. VERDICT: the stability-vs-strong-competition trade-off is confirmed across SIX levers (founder, A-strength, regen, bump geometry, moving patch, energy buffer) and shown cleanly by the A on/off contrast: resource-limited <=> strong competition <=> oscillatory; A-stabilized <=> damped <=> weak competition — never both. The trade-off is A-driven and fundamental to this consumer-resource architecture, NOT an artifact of the energy buffer. Continuous-locomotion chapter robustly closed. The TRUE no-buffer escape (instantaneous-intake demography with NO energy state at all) remains technically untested but with a weakened premise (oscillation survived a near-zero buffer); a genuine agent-architecture redesign, re-open on a word.
- Honest caveat: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT. Config-only probe; only energy_capacity swept (four values). The TRUE no-buffer escape (zero energy state, not just a low cap) remains technically untested but its premise is now weakened (oscillation survived near-zero buffer). Self-grade: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT.
- Verdict: NEGATIVE / NEW INSIGHT. No-energy-buffer escape fails. The stability-vs-strong-competition trade-off is A-driven and confirmed across SIX levers; continuous-locomotion chapter robustly closed.
- Verifier: controller directly ran the A-OFF/A-ON x energy_capacity sweep + the A-OFF field-availability check (raw numbers); strong A-OFF competition (corr -0.91) vs weak A-ON (~6%) at fixed regime, CV flat across buffer sizes — density-dependence (not the whole-field availability proxy, Exp 244) is the binding arbiter.
- trace: experiments/exp247_no_buffer.py -> experiments/outputs/exp247.txt

## Exp 248 — Red Queen predator-prey: CAN'T-POSE at the viability gate; no two-trophic coexistence on this substrate (NEGATIVE / CONSOLIDATION — extends the stability-vs-competition trade-off to a second trophic level). Direction: co-evolution / environmental-complexity.
- Plain: The named structural escape from the local-gradient wall was a CO-EVOLVING antagonist (a predator provides a non-saturating selection gradient that a static resource cannot).
